[20]Gut Microbiota Alterations in Patients with Panic Disorder: A Case-Control Study. PMC. 2025. PMC12430356. — Panic disorder-specific gut microbiome research — the most directly relevant emerging study.

What Is Chazza Chi — And Why Wingmen Need to Understand It

Thirty-five years. That's how long I've lived with panic disorder (a condition where the brain's alarm system misfires and sends the whole body into a full emergency response — racing heart, tunnel vision, intense fear — even when there is zero actual danger). Not read about it. Not studied it. Lived it — the racing heart, the tunnel vision, the way a perfectly ordinary Tuesday afternoon can flip into a full-body emergency in about twelve seconds flat. What I learned, mostly by accident, is that the people around me had more influence over my nervous system's baseline state (how wound-up or how calm my body runs on any given day) than I ever gave them credit for. Not because they had magical powers. Because environment matters. What you eat matters. Whether you move matters. And the people who love someone with panic disorder are shaping all three of those things every single day — whether they know it or not. This is Chazza Chi. This is what Anxiety Wingman is built on.
6M+Americans living with panic disorder right now — 2.7% of the U.S. population in any given year [1]
167hours per week that belong to the wingman — not the therapist. One hour of therapy leaves 167 hours where you are the recovery environment [2]
54–86%improvement in recovery rates when family members and partners are actively educated and involved in treatment [3]
88%of Al-Anon (a support program for family members — more on this below) members report their own mental health improved from learning to support differently [4]

First — What panic disorder actually is

Let's start here because most people — even people who have been living with someone who has panic disorder for years — don't fully understand what's happening inside that person's body. And that misunderstanding is exactly where most of the mistakes happen.

Panic disorder is not "bad anxiety." It is not just "being stressed out." It is not a character flaw, a weakness, or something the person could stop if they really wanted to. Panic disorder is a chronic (long-lasting) dysregulation (misfiring) of the autonomic nervous system (ANS — the part of your nervous system that controls automatic body functions like heart rate, breathing, and the emergency stress response). [5]

Here is what that means in plain language. Your brain has a built-in alarm system. Think of it like a smoke detector in your kitchen. That smoke detector's job is to keep you safe — to fire a loud alarm the moment it detects smoke, so you can get out of the house before you're hurt. In most people, that alarm system fires when there is actual smoke. In a person with panic disorder, that alarm system fires when someone burns toast. Or when the light hits a weird angle. Or when the person's heart beats slightly differently for just one second. The alarm has been set way too sensitive — and the body responds to the alarm as if the house is genuinely on fire every single time. [6]

The specific part of the brain that acts as this alarm system is called the amygdala (pronounced uh-MIG-duh-luh — a small, almond-shaped area deep in the brain that acts as your personal danger detector, scanning your world and your body 24 hours a day for anything that might be a threat). When the amygdala gets calibrated (set) too sensitive, it fires the full emergency alarm — the full fight-or-flight response (the body's emergency system that floods you with adrenaline — a powerful hormone — and cortisol — the main stress hormone — to prepare your body to either fight a threat or escape from it) — for things that are not actually dangerous. A crowded store. Sitting in traffic. The way your heart beat just a little differently for a second. [7]

The key insight: The nervous system (your body's entire communication network — brain, spinal cord, and all the nerves running throughout your body) is trainable in both directions. You can accidentally train it toward hypervigilance (being permanently stuck in high-alert mode, constantly bracing for danger even when none is present) over years. You can also — slowly, systematically — train it back toward calm. That's what Chazza Chi is about. [8]

What a panic attack actually feels like — from the inside

Before we go any further, let's make sure you actually understand what a panic attack is. Not as a clinical (medical) term. As a human experience happening to someone you love. Because if you don't understand what's happening inside them, you genuinely cannot know how to help — and you will keep accidentally making things worse with the best of intentions.

A panic attack is not a tantrum. It is not attention-seeking. It is not dramatic or exaggerated. A panic attack is your body genuinely believing it is about to die — and responding with everything it has. [6]

Here is the sequence of what happens inside the body during a panic attack, step by step. The amygdala (the brain's danger detector) fires the emergency signal. Within seconds — not minutes, seconds — the adrenal glands (small, triangular organs that sit on top of the kidneys) flood the entire bloodstream with adrenaline (a powerful emergency hormone that makes your heart race, your breathing speed up, and your muscles tense — also called epinephrine) and cortisol (the body's main stress hormone that puts and keeps the body on high alert — chronically high cortisol from repeated panic attacks damages the gut, immune system, and nervous system over time). [9]

The heart rate shoots up — often to 150, 160, even 180 beats per minute. Breathing becomes fast and shallow. The blood vessels in the hands and feet constrict (tighten up) so blood can rush to the large muscle groups — which is exactly why hands tingle or go numb during a panic attack. Vision narrows to a tunnel. The digestive system shuts down and redirects blood away from the stomach — which is why stomach pain, nausea, and diarrhea are common panic symptoms. Derealization (the feeling that the world isn't real — like being in a dream) and depersonalization (the feeling that you yourself aren't real — like watching yourself from a distance) are also common and deeply frightening. The whole body is screaming: Danger. Run. Fight. Get out. [10]

And then — this is the part that traps people and keeps them trapped for years — the brain logs the entire experience. It records the location, the time of day, the smells, the sounds, the physical sensations — everything about where and when the alarm fired. It adds all of that to a growing internal list of "dangerous" things and places to avoid next time. This is called conditioned fear learning (the process where the brain learns to be afraid of things that were simply nearby when panic struck, even if those things were harmless). The person's world gets smaller. [11]

Why this matters for you as a wingman: When the person you love avoids a place, a situation, or an activity — they are not being lazy, dramatic, or manipulative. Their brain has genuinely filed that thing under "dangerous." The avoidance makes complete logical sense from inside their nervous system. Understanding this — really understanding it — is the beginning of being actually helpful, instead of accidentally making things worse out of love and frustration.

The panic cycle — why it doesn't just get better on its own

Here is why panic disorder doesn't just go away on its own if the person tries hard enough or thinks positive thoughts. There is a self-reinforcing cycle (a loop that keeps itself going) built into the disorder itself — and without understanding the cycle, most attempts to help accidentally feed it and make it stronger. [12]

Step one: A panic attack happens somewhere. The body goes through the full emergency response. It ends — panic attacks always end, usually within 10 to 30 minutes. The person is left exhausted, shaken, and embarrassed.

Step two: The brain adds the location and sensations to the threat map. Now that place or that physical sensation feels dangerous — even on days when no panic attack actually happens there.

Step three: The person starts watching their own body constantly — monitoring their heart rate, breathing, the feeling in their chest, any sensation that might mean another panic attack is coming. This is called hypervigilance to interoceptive cues (constantly scanning the inside of your own body for any sensation that might signal a panic attack is coming — 'interoceptive' means relating to feelings from inside the body). [13]

Step four: Because the person is watching their body so closely, they notice every tiny blip — every slightly fast heartbeat, every odd stomach feeling, every moment of lightheadedness. These sensations happen to everyone's body all day long. They are completely normal. But for the person with panic disorder, each one triggers a spike of alarm. The alarm raises the heart rate. The raised heart rate confirms the alarm. The alarm escalates. Full panic attack — triggered by a completely normal heartbeat. [6]

Step five: The person avoids more things. Stays closer to home. Stops going to places they used to love. Stops doing activities they used to enjoy. And with every avoidance, the brain receives one more piece of evidence: yes, that was dangerous. Good thing we stayed away. The world gets smaller and smaller. [11]

Step six: Everyone around the person — partners, parents, siblings, close friends — starts quietly adjusting their own lives to work around the disorder. They stop suggesting activities that seem hard. They give in when the person says they can't make it somewhere. They provide constant reassurance. They take over responsibilities the person has stopped being able to manage. And with every one of these accommodations (changes in behavior to work around the disorder), the brain gets one more piece of evidence: yes, those situations were too dangerous to handle. Good thing someone helped me avoid them. [14]

This is the part that is hardest to hear, and the most important part of this entire document: The instinctive, loving, human impulse to protect someone you care about — to make things easier, to take the hard things off their plate, to say "it's okay, you don't have to go" — is the exact thing that makes panic disorder worse over time. Not because your love is wrong. Because the disorder feeds on avoidance, and every accommodation confirms that avoidance was the right call. The most helpful thing you can do is deeply counterintuitive. This document is about teaching you what that looks like.

The 167-hour principle — why the wingman is the most important person in recovery

There are 168 hours in a week. A therapist, if the person is even seeing one, gets one of those hours. Maybe two or three if treatment is especially intensive. That leaves 165 to 167 hours — every night of sleep, every meal, every morning routine, every drive to the store, every evening at home — where the person's recovery environment is controlled not by a trained clinician (a licensed mental health professional), but by the people they live with and love. [2]

The research on what this means for outcomes is not ambiguous. Family members who understand panic disorder and respond to it correctly produce measurably better outcomes than family members who don't — even when the clinical treatment the person receives is exactly the same. [3] Studies of partner-assisted CBT (Cognitive Behavioral Therapy — a type of therapy that helps people change unhelpful thoughts and gradually face feared situations instead of avoiding them, and the most well-studied treatment for panic disorder) for agoraphobia (a condition that often develops alongside panic disorder where the person becomes afraid of being in places where escape would be difficult if a panic attack hit) show that when partners are educated and actively involved, recovery rates improve by 54 to 86 percent compared to individual therapy alone. [3]

That is not a small difference. That is often the difference between getting better and not getting better. The wingman is not a supporting character in this story. The wingman is part of the treatment — whether or not they realize it, and whether or not the person is even in therapy at all.

Where Anxiety Wingman comes from — the Al-Anon model: Al-Anon (a worldwide support program — founded in 1951 — specifically for family members of people with alcohol problems, built on the idea that the family members themselves need education and support, not just the person with the disorder) figured this out seventy years ago: the people around the person in crisis need their own support, their own education, and their own framework. They are not the patient — but they are absolutely part of the treatment environment. And when they learn to respond differently, outcomes change — for everyone in the family, not just the person with the disorder. Anxiety Wingman operates on the exact same principle, applied specifically to panic disorder. [4]

The triangle — why three pillars, and why a triangle

The triangle is the strongest geometric (shape-based) structure humans have ever figured out how to build with. Look at any bridge and you will see triangles in the trusses (the structural framework underneath). Look at any stable roof and you will see triangles in the rafters. Look at any tower that has stood for centuries and you will see triangles in its design. A triangle cannot collapse by pushing on one corner — the force spreads equally across all three sides. Push one corner down and the other two automatically push back. [15]

That is exactly what Chazza Chi does for a nervous system that panic disorder has put under enormous structural stress.

Three pillars. Three sides. Work all three and you build something that holds up under real-world pressure — the kind of pressure panic disorder puts on a person and everyone around them, every single day. Remove one pillar completely and the other two still provide partial support — but the structure weakens significantly. Work all three and you get what engineers call redundancy (when multiple systems each do part of the same job, so if one slips temporarily, the others hold the structure steady). [15]

Pillar 1 — Nutrition & Gut Health

Feed the gut well. The gut is where the body makes most of its neurotransmitters (the chemical messengers that regulate mood, anxiety, and the nervous system — including serotonin, GABA, and dopamine, most of which are actually made in the gut). No healthy gut, no chemical foundation for calm. The food in the house shapes the nervous system of everyone living in it — whether anyone knows it or not.

Pillar 2 — Movement & Nervous System

Give the nervous system somewhere to discharge (release and burn off) stress hormones. Build real brake power in the body's calming system. Stimulate the vagus nerve (the body's longest nerve — running from the brain all the way to the gut — that activates the body's calming system when stimulated by movement, breathing, and certain foods). Movement is not a lifestyle suggestion for panic disorder. It is a direct neurological (brain and nervous system) intervention.

Pillar 3 — Environment & Sensory Design

Sustain the calm state the other two pillars build. Continuous (ongoing, all-day) sensory management across five channels — scent, sound, light, physical sensation, and air quality — without the person needing to know anything is different. The wingman adjusts the environment the way a good HVAC (heating, ventilation, and air conditioning) system adjusts temperature — automatically, quietly, and continuously, without anyone having to think about it.

Why wingmen — and what that role actually means

The word "wingman" comes from military aviation. In a two-plane formation, the wingman's job is to fly beside and slightly behind the lead pilot — protecting blind spots, watching for threats the lead can't see, and covering the lead so they can focus on flying the mission. The wingman doesn't fly the lead's plane. Doesn't take over the controls. Doesn't tell the lead how to fly. They do their own job — consistently, reliably, without drama — so the lead pilot can do theirs. [16]

That is the role. Not manager. Not fixer. Not protector who takes all the hard things away. The person flying alongside — doing their job — so the lead can fly.

Being a wingman for someone with panic disorder is not about managing them. It is not about telling them to calm down — which never, ever works and usually makes things worse. It is not about walking on eggshells. It is not about giving up your own life or your own wellbeing to orbit theirs. It is about learning to adjust the environment around them — the way you'd turn down a thermostat or dim a lamp. Small. Unobtrusive (not drawing attention to itself). Continuous. And mostly invisible. [17]

Most people who love someone with panic disorder are already doing something — they are already adjusting, already accommodating (bending their own behavior around the disorder), already trying. The problem is not effort. The problem is direction. Most instinctive helping behaviors — constant reassurance, doing difficult things for the person, rearranging the whole family's life around the disorder — make panic disorder worse over time, not better. The research on this pattern is consistent and clear across five decades of study. [14]

This document is about learning what actually helps — and then consistently doing that instead, even when it feels counterintuitive.

What a wingman does

Adjusts the sensory environment quietly and without announcement. Introduces food changes gradually as normal household shifts. Finds low-key movement invitations that don't feel like therapy or homework. Notices what seems to help and quietly does more of it. Maintains their own calm, regulated state — because the wingman's nervous system directly influences the nervous system of the person with panic disorder through a process called co-regulation (more on this in the Wingman Framework article).

What a wingman doesn't do

Doesn't diagnose, prescribe, push, manage, enable avoidance, or try to fix something that genuinely isn't theirs to fix. Doesn't walk on eggshells or sacrifice their own mental health and wellbeing. Doesn't give constant reassurance (it makes the disorder stronger). Doesn't pretend it isn't hard or exhausting. Supports the system. Trusts the person to own their own recovery path.

Why this approach works

Because nervous systems respond to environment whether or not the person consciously participates or even knows what's happening. The food changes work even if the person doesn't know about them. The lighting changes work even if the person doesn't notice. The calm in the wingman's voice works even if the person doesn't understand why the house feels different. You don't have to announce what you're doing for it to work. The nervous system responds anyway.

How the three pillars connect — and why the triangle is stronger than any single side

The three pillars are not three separate tracks running on separate rails. They feed each other constantly, in both directions, all day long. That interaction — that back-and-forth between pillars — is where most of the power actually comes from. [15]

A morning walk in nature is Pillar 2 (movement — it releases BDNF — Brain-Derived Neurotrophic Factor, a protein that acts like fertilizer for brain cells and helps them grow and repair — BDNF is one of the key reasons why exercise directly improves anxiety and stimulates the vagus nerve — the body's main calming nerve) and Pillar 3 (environment — natural outdoor settings calm the amygdala in documented, measurable ways that urban environments simply do not) simultaneously. You get two full pillars of benefit from one simple activity. [18]

A meal cooked with medicinal spices (everyday cooking spices like turmeric, ginger, rosemary, and cinnamon whose active compounds have been shown in research to reduce brain inflammation and support nervous system health), eaten slowly in a calm room with good warm light and soft background music, hits all three pillars at once — gut health and neurotransmitter support, nervous system regulation through the slow eating pace, and environmental calming through light and sound. The pillars stack their benefits on top of each other. [19]

That stacking and compounding — where two pillars working together produce a third-pillar benefit automatically — is what makes the triangle structurally different from any single approach. It is not three separate to-do lists. It is one integrated (all working together) system, designed to compound over time the way interest compounds in a bank account. Small, consistent actions in any pillar make the other two easier. Over weeks and months, the trajectory of the nervous system shifts. [15]

The wingman's job in one sentence: Learn the three pillars, apply them quietly and consistently, and trust that the nervous system responds to environment whether or not the person knows you're doing it. You are the thermostat. You adjust the environment around the person. You do not adjust the person. The thermostat does not argue with the room about what temperature it should be. It simply adjusts — and the room changes.

How to use this document

This is not a book you read cover to cover in one sitting and then set aside. It is a reference — a living resource you return to at different points across the recovery journey. Read this Foundation overview first. Then go to whichever pillar feels most immediately relevant to where you are right now. Come back to the others when you are ready. Return to any article when something in your situation changes.

Every article in this hub is built on the same principles:

  • The research is cited. Every significant claim has a source you can look up and read yourself. The citations are listed at the bottom of each article. You do not have to take any of this on faith.
  • The language is plain. Technical terms are defined right where they appear — in parentheses, or via the underlined terms (hover your mouse over any underlined word or phrase to see a plain-language definition appear). You should never need to open a second browser tab to understand what a word means.
  • The tone is honest. Some of what you will read in this document is difficult. The truth about panic disorder and about family accommodation is not always comfortable. But understanding the truth is the only foundation for actually helping.
  • The focus is always on you — the wingman. What can you do? What should you stop doing? How does your own physical and emotional state affect theirs? Every article is built for the person doing the supporting — because that person is you.

One last thing before you go deeper: You did not cause this. You cannot cure this. But you can absolutely make it better or significantly worse by how you show up every day — in the kitchen, in the living room, on a walk, in the moment when panic strikes. The fact that you are here, reading this, trying to understand — that already puts you in the category of wingmen the research shows produce better outcomes. Keep going.

★
Reference
Quick Reference Glossary — Every Technical Term Used in This Document, Defined in Plain Language
▾

This glossary defines every technical, medical, and scientific term used throughout the Anxiety Wingman Hub. If you encounter a word in any article and want a plain-language definition, find it here. Terms are listed alphabetically.

A

Acetylcholine: A neurotransmitter (chemical messenger between nerve cells) that activates muscles and is involved in many autonomic nervous system functions. Linalool (the main active compound in lavender) inhibits excessive acetylcholine release, producing smooth muscle relaxation and reducing physical tension.

Adrenaline (Epinephrine): A powerful hormone produced by the adrenal glands (small organs sitting on top of the kidneys) in response to stress or danger. Adrenaline speeds up the heart rate, raises blood pressure, opens airways, redirects blood to muscles, and prepares the body to fight or flee. In panic disorder, adrenaline is released in response to imagined rather than real threats — producing the physical sensations of a panic attack (racing heart, fast breathing, trembling).

Adrenal glands: Two small, triangular-shaped glands that sit on top of the kidneys. They produce adrenaline (epinephrine), cortisol, and other stress hormones in response to signals from the brain. In panic disorder, the adrenal glands are called into action too frequently by an oversensitive threat-detection system.

Afferent (vagal afferent neurons): "Afferent" means moving toward the center — toward the brain. Vagal afferent neurons are the nerve cells in the vagus nerve that carry signals from the gut upward to the brain. About 80% of vagus nerve fibers are afferent — meaning the gut is doing most of the "talking" in the gut-brain relationship.

Agoraphobia: A condition that frequently develops alongside panic disorder, where the person becomes afraid of situations where escape would be difficult or help unavailable if a panic attack struck. This can include open spaces, crowds, public transportation, bridges, being outside the home alone, or even just leaving the immediate neighborhood. In severe cases, agoraphobia makes a person unable to leave their home at all. Pronounced ag-oh-ruh-FOE-bee-uh.

Allostasis: The process by which the body achieves stability through change — constantly adjusting its internal state in response to anticipated demands. "Allostatic load" refers to the accumulated wear and tear on the body from chronic stress and repeated activation of the stress response.

Alpha waves (brainwave state): Electrical oscillations in the brain occurring at 8-13 Hz (cycles per second) — associated with relaxed, calm alertness. Alpha waves are present during relaxed wakefulness and light meditation, when the person is calm but not drowsy. Alpha-range binaural beats (sound frequencies played through headphones) can partially guide the brain toward this state.

Amygdala: Pronounced uh-MIG-duh-luh. A small, almond-shaped cluster of nerve cells deep inside the brain. The amygdala's main job is to scan all incoming sensory information for potential threats, and to trigger the emergency fight-or-flight response when it detects something threatening. In panic disorder, the amygdala is chronically oversensitive — it fires the alarm too easily, for things that aren't actually dangerous. Walking, exercise, and environmental calming all measurably reduce amygdala reactivity over time.

ANS (Autonomic Nervous System): The part of the nervous system that controls automatic body functions — things the body does without conscious direction. Includes heart rate, breathing, digestion, hormone release, and the stress response. Has two main branches: the sympathetic nervous system (fight-or-flight — the emergency stress system) and the parasympathetic nervous system (rest-and-digest — the calming and recovery system). In panic disorder, the sympathetic branch is chronically overactive and the parasympathetic branch is chronically underactive.

B

BDNF (Brain-Derived Neurotrophic Factor): A protein in the brain often described as "fertilizer for brain cells." BDNF helps existing brain cells grow new connections, helps the brain repair itself, and is essential for the brain's ability to regulate emotions and anxiety. People with panic disorder consistently have below-average BDNF levels. Exercise — especially sustained aerobic exercise like brisk walking — is the most powerful known way to stimulate BDNF production. Regular exercise raises BDNF, which directly improves the brain's capacity to regulate anxiety over weeks and months.

Benzodiazepines: A class of prescription medications used for anxiety, panic attacks, and insomnia. Examples include alprazolam (Xanax), diazepam (Valium), lorazepam (Ativan), and clonazepam (Klonopin). They work by strongly enhancing GABA-A receptor activity — boosting the brain's main calming chemical system. Effective for acute panic attacks but have significant downsides including risk of physical dependence and tolerance (needing more over time for the same effect).

Binaural beats: Pronounced by-NOH-ruh-ul. An auditory (hearing-based) phenomenon created when two tones of slightly different frequencies are delivered separately to each ear through headphones. The brain perceives a single pulsing "beat" at the difference between the two frequencies — and can be partially guided toward different states (calm alertness, deep relaxation, or sleep) based on that perceived frequency. Require headphones to work — don't work through speakers.

Bidirectional: Working or flowing in both directions at the same time. The gut-brain axis is bidirectional — the gut sends signals to the brain AND the brain sends signals to the gut, simultaneously and continuously.

BPM (Beats Per Minute): A standard measurement of: (1) musical tempo — how fast the beat of a piece of music plays, and (2) heart rate — how many times the heart beats per minute. A resting adult heart rate is typically 60-80 BPM. Music at approximately 60 BPM or slower tends to synchronize with and slow the heart rate through a process called entrainment.

C

CBT (Cognitive Behavioral Therapy): A well-researched type of therapy that works by helping people identify and change unhelpful thinking patterns (the "cognitive" or thought-related part) and gradually face feared situations instead of avoiding them (the "behavioral" or action-related part). CBT is considered the gold-standard, most effective treatment for panic disorder by major clinical guidelines worldwide. Most effective when partner-assisted — when the wingman understands and supports the CBT approach.

Chemoreceptors: Specialized sensory cells in the body that detect chemical changes in the blood — specifically changes in oxygen, carbon dioxide, and blood acidity levels. In the brainstem (the lower part of the brain where it connects to the spinal cord), chemoreceptors continuously monitor blood CO₂ levels. When CO₂ rises, they trigger increased breathing. In panic disorder, these receptors are abnormally sensitive — they can trigger anxiety responses at CO₂ levels that wouldn't bother most people. This is why indoor CO₂ elevation from poor ventilation can worsen panic disorder symptoms.

Chronic: Ongoing, persistent, and long-lasting — typically for more than three months. "Chronic" is the opposite of "acute" (which means sudden and short-lived). Panic disorder is a chronic condition — it persists over time and requires ongoing management rather than a single cure.

Circadian rhythm: The body's internal 24-hour biological clock — regulating when cortisol peaks and falls, when melatonin rises for sleep, when body temperature fluctuates, when digestion is most active, and dozens of other physiological processes. Morning outdoor light is the primary signal that anchors this clock daily. Disrupted circadian rhythms (from irregular light exposure, shift work, or jet lag) worsen anxiety and panic disorder.

CNS (Central Nervous System): The brain and spinal cord together. The command center of the entire nervous system — receives information from the body, processes it, and sends instructions back out. In panic disorder, the CNS is running with a miscalibrated threat-detection system that fires emergency responses too easily and too often.

CO₂ (Carbon Dioxide): The gas produced when the body uses oxygen — exhaled with every breath. In well-ventilated spaces, outdoor air keeps indoor CO₂ near the natural baseline of about 400 ppm. In closed rooms, CO₂ builds to 1,000-2,500 ppm — a level that measurably impairs thinking and, in people with panic disorder's documented CO₂ hypersensitivity, elevates anxiety. CO₂ is detected by brainstem chemoreceptors that are overly sensitive in panic disorder.

Co-regulation: The biological process by which one person's regulated (calm) nervous system helps another person's nervous system calm down — through physical presence, calm touch, tone of voice, and proximity. Co-regulation is the neurological foundation of what happens when a calm, warm wingman sits close to someone having a panic attack. Their regulated nervous system exerts a real, measurable calming influence on the dysregulated one.

Conditioned fear learning: The process by which the brain learns to be afraid of things that were simply nearby when panic struck, even if those things were harmless. After a panic attack in a grocery store, the grocery store becomes coded as "dangerous" in the brain's threat map — even though nothing in the store is actually threatening. Over time, the list of conditioned "dangerous" things grows, and the person's world gets smaller as they avoid more and more.

Cortisol: Often called "the stress hormone." Produced by the adrenal glands in response to stress. Cortisol raises blood sugar to give the body quick energy, suppresses the immune system temporarily to divert resources to muscles, and keeps the body in a state of high alert. Chronically elevated cortisol — from ongoing anxiety and repeated panic attacks — damages the gut lining, impairs sleep quality, suppresses immune function, and directly worsens anxiety neurochemistry. Multiple Pillar 1, 2, and 3 interventions work partly by reducing chronic cortisol levels.

Cytokines: Pronounced SY-toh-kynes. Small proteins released by immune cells that act as chemical signals to coordinate immune responses. When gut bacteria are unhealthy (dysbiosis) or the gut lining is damaged, immune cells produce inflammatory cytokines that can travel to the brain and directly worsen mood, anxiety, and cognitive function.

D

Default Mode Network (DMN): A network of brain areas that becomes most active when the mind is not focused on an external task — during mind-wandering, self-referential thinking, and rumination (going over worries repeatedly). In anxiety disorders, the DMN tends to generate anxious, threat-focused content rather than neutral mind-wandering. Flow states (deeply absorbed activity) and formal meditation both deactivate the DMN, giving it a rest from anxiety-generating content.

Delta waves (brainwave state): Electrical oscillations in the brain occurring at less than 4 Hz (cycles per second) — associated with deep sleep. Delta-range binaural beats may help induce sleep onset in people with insomnia.

Depersonalization: A feeling of being detached from one's own mental processes or body — like watching yourself from a distance, or feeling like you are not real. A common panic attack symptom caused by the emergency nervous system response.

Derealization: A feeling that the surrounding world is unreal, distant, or somehow strange and dream-like. Another common panic attack symptom, often occurring alongside depersonalization, caused by the same emergency nervous system response.

Diaphragmatic breathing: Breathing using the diaphragm — the large dome-shaped muscle that separates the chest from the abdomen. When the diaphragm contracts and moves downward, the belly expands and the lungs fill deeply. Diaphragmatic breathing directly stimulates the vagus nerve (the body's main calming nerve) through mechanical contact, activating the parasympathetic (rest-and-calm) system. Most people breathe shallowly from the chest at rest, which is less effective and maintains a mild stress-system activation.

DMN: See "Default Mode Network."

Dopamine: A neurotransmitter (brain chemical) that regulates motivation, reward, pleasure, and movement. Often associated with the "reward circuit" of the brain — dopamine is released when something pleasurable or rewarding happens, reinforcing behaviors. About 50% of the body's dopamine is produced in the gut. Chronic stress, poor diet, and gut dysbiosis can lower dopamine availability, reducing motivation and increasing the emotional weight of anxiety.

DPS (Deep Pressure Stimulation): Firm, distributed, sustained pressure applied to a large area of the body — such as the weight of a weighted blanket, the pressure of compression clothing, or the sensation of a firm hug. DPS activates mechanoreceptors (pressure-sensing nerve cells) in the skin and connective tissue that send "settled and safe" signals to the nervous system, shifting it from sympathetic (fight-or-flight) dominance toward parasympathetic (rest-and-calm) dominance.

Dysbiosis: Pronounced dis-by-OH-sis. An imbalance in the gut microbiome — the community of bacteria, fungi, and other microorganisms living in the digestive tract. Dysbiosis means the gut has too many harmful bacteria and not enough beneficial ones, or has lost the diversity it needs to function well. Dysbiosis reduces serotonin production in the gut, disrupts vagal signaling, promotes inflammation, and is strongly linked to anxiety, depression, and gut disorders like IBS.

Dysregulated / Dysregulation: Not working the way it should — misfiring, overreacting, or underreacting. A dysregulated nervous system is one that fires emergency responses too easily (panic attacks from harmless stimuli), has difficulty recovering from stress (low HRV), and maintains a chronically elevated baseline activation level (always on edge). Dysregulation is the central feature of panic disorder.

E

Emulsifiers: Chemical compounds used in processed foods to keep ingredients mixed together smoothly and extend shelf life. Common examples include carrageenan, polysorbate 80, carboxymethylcellulose, and lecithin. Research in animal models has shown that certain emulsifiers (particularly polysorbate 80 and carboxymethylcellulose) damage the gut mucus layer and promote intestinal permeability ("leaky gut"), triggering inflammation that affects the brain and nervous system.

ENS (Enteric Nervous System): The nervous system embedded directly in the walls of the digestive tract. "Enteric" means relating to the intestines. The ENS contains 100 to 500 million nerve cells — more than the entire spinal cord — and can function completely independently of the brain. Scientists call it the "second brain." In panic disorder, a disrupted ENS sends distress signals upward to the brain that amplify the anxiety and panic response.

Entrainment: When one rhythmically oscillating system (like a heartbeat) falls into synchrony with another (like a musical beat or binaural beat frequency). The heart, which is responsive to autonomic nervous system input, will naturally tend to match its rate to a steady external rhythm — which is how slow music literally slows the heart rate, and how resonant breathing at 6 breaths per minute synchronizes the heart rate with the breath cycle.

Expressed Emotion (EE): A research measure of the emotional attitudes expressed by family members toward a person with a mental health condition. High expressed emotion includes high levels of criticism (direct expressions of displeasure), hostility (generalized rejection), or emotional overinvolvement (excessive self-sacrifice, over-protectiveness). Low expressed emotion means warm, calm, non-reactive support. Decades of research across anxiety, depression, and schizophrenia consistently show that high expressed emotion in family members predicts worse mental health outcomes.

F-G

Fight-or-flight response: The body's built-in emergency system — developed over millions of years of evolution to protect humans from physical danger. When the brain signals "danger," the body floods with adrenaline and cortisol, the heart races, breathing speeds up, muscles tense, and vision narrows. Everything is preparing to either fight the threat or escape from it. In panic disorder, this entire emergency response fires when there is no actual physical threat.

Flow state: A mental state of complete absorption in a challenging but manageable activity — where self-consciousness fades, time distorts, and the brain's default mode network (rumination and self-focused anxiety) goes quiet. Neurologically associated with reduced amygdala activation and suppressed threat monitoring. Activities that produce flow states — woodworking, fishing, cooking, playing music, gardening — provide real neurological rest for the anxious brain.

GABA (Gamma-Aminobutyric Acid): Pronounced GAM-uh uh-MEE-noh-byoo-TIR-ik ASS-id. The brain's primary inhibitory (calming, slowing) neurotransmitter — the main chemical that suppresses excessive nervous system activity. When GABA is active, nerve cells are less likely to fire, reducing anxiety. Benzodiazepine medications (Xanax, Valium, Klonopin) work by strongly enhancing GABA activity. Certain gut bacteria, especially Lactobacillus and Bifidobacterium strains, also produce GABA directly. In panic disorder, the GABA system is chronically underactive — the brain's natural brake pedal isn't working well enough.

GAD (Generalized Anxiety Disorder): A chronic anxiety condition involving persistent, excessive, and difficult-to-control worrying about many different topics — not specific to any one situation. GAD and panic disorder frequently occur together (they are comorbid — meaning they co-occur). Both involve dysregulation of the autonomic nervous system and the HPA axis (the brain-hormone stress response system).

Ghrelin: Often called "the hunger hormone." Produced primarily in the stomach. Ghrelin levels rise before meals and fall after eating. Ghrelin also plays a role in stress and anxiety responses — elevated ghrelin is associated with increased anxiety, and chronic stress disrupts normal ghrelin patterns.

GI (Gastrointestinal): Relating to the digestive system — specifically the stomach (gastro-) and intestines (-intestinal). "GI tract" means the entire digestive system from the esophagus (food tube) through the stomach and intestines to the colon and rectum. The GI tract is where food is broken down, nutrients are absorbed, and — critically for anxiety — where most of the body's serotonin and other neurotransmitters are made.

GERD (Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease): A digestive condition where stomach acid regularly flows backward up into the esophagus (the food tube from the mouth to the stomach), causing heartburn, chest pain, and other symptoms. GERD is more common in people with anxiety disorders and is thought to be partly driven by the same autonomic nervous system dysfunction that underlies panic disorder. Pronounced gastro-eh-SOF-uh-JEE-ul REE-flux disease.

Gut microbiome: The community of trillions of bacteria, viruses, fungi, and other microorganisms living in the human digestive system — primarily in the large intestine. The microbiome helps digest food, trains the immune system, produces neurotransmitters (including serotonin and GABA), and maintains the gut lining's health. An unhealthy or imbalanced microbiome (dysbiosis) is linked to anxiety, depression, and many inflammatory conditions.

H

HEPA (High Efficiency Particulate Air): A filter standard that captures 99.97% of airborne particles 0.3 micrometers or larger — including dust, pollen, mold spores, bacteria, and PM2.5 particles. HEPA filters are used in air purifiers to improve indoor air quality. They address particles and some VOCs but do NOT remove CO₂ — ventilation (open windows) is still needed for CO₂ management.

HPA axis (Hypothalamic-Pituitary-Adrenal axis): The three-part hormonal system that controls the body's stress response. When the brain perceives a threat, the hypothalamus (a brain region) signals the pituitary gland (in the brain) to signal the adrenal glands (on top of the kidneys) to release cortisol (the main stress hormone). In panic disorder, this system is chronically over-activated. Gut microbiome composition directly influences how sensitive and reactive the HPA axis is.

HRV (Heart Rate Variability): The natural variation in time between consecutive heartbeats. A healthy heart does NOT beat with mechanical precision — there are natural slight variations between beats that reflect how well the autonomic nervous system is functioning. Higher HRV = better nervous system flexibility and resilience = better ability to shift between stress and recovery states. Lower HRV = more rigid nervous system = difficulty recovering from stress. People with panic disorder consistently have lower HRV than people without it. HRV can be improved through exercise, breathing practices, diet, and environmental changes.

Hypervigilance: A state of being constantly on high alert — always scanning for danger, always braced for something bad to happen. The nervous system is running in emergency mode continuously, even when there is no actual emergency. Hypervigilance is exhausting and is one of the defining features of panic disorder. Hypervigilance to interoceptive cues means constantly monitoring internal body sensations (heartbeat, breathing, stomach feelings) for any sign that a panic attack might be coming.

I-L

IBS (Irritable Bowel Syndrome): A very common digestive condition causing abdominal pain, bloating, cramping, and unpredictable bowel habits (diarrhea, constipation, or alternating between both) without any visible structural damage to the gut when examined. IBS is strongly associated with anxiety disorders — both share the same underlying gut-brain axis dysregulation. IBS affects an estimated 10-15% of the worldwide population.

Inhibitory neurotransmitter: A brain chemical that slows down or suppresses nerve cell activity — reducing the likelihood that nerve cells will fire alarm signals. GABA is the brain's main inhibitory neurotransmitter. In panic disorder, the brain's inhibitory system is chronically underactive — the brake pedal doesn't work well enough to stop anxiety from escalating.

Interoceptive cues: Sensations from inside the body — heartbeat, breathing rate and depth, temperature changes, gut sensations, muscle tension, dizziness. In panic disorder, people are hypervigilant to interoceptive cues — constantly monitoring these internal signals for anything that might indicate a panic attack is coming. When they find a small variation (a slightly faster heartbeat, a stomach feeling), the alarm fires — even if the sensation was completely harmless.

Linalool: Pronounced LIN-uh-lool. The primary active chemical compound in lavender essential oil — also found in smaller amounts in basil, rosemary, coriander, and some citrus peels. Linalool is responsible for most of lavender's documented effects on the nervous system: it relaxes smooth muscles, enhances GABA-A receptor activity (the brain's calming system), and reduces the ease with which nerve cells fire panic-escalating signals. Its effects are pharmacological (drug-like), not merely psychological.

M-N

Mechanoreceptors: Specialized sensory nerve cells in the skin and connective tissue that detect physical pressure, touch, and vibration. Different types respond to different kinds of pressure — some to light touch, some to deep sustained pressure like a weighted blanket. Deep pressure mechanoreceptors, when stimulated continuously, send "safe and settled" signals to the brain's autonomic control centers, shifting the nervous system toward calm.

Melatonin: A hormone produced by the pineal gland (a small gland in the brain) primarily in response to darkness. Melatonin is the body's main sleep signal — its rise in the evening tells the body it's time to sleep. Blue-wavelength light from screens and LED lighting suppresses melatonin production, delaying sleep onset. Weighted blankets and warm evening environments support timely melatonin production by maintaining comfortable conditions for sleep transition.

Microbiome: See "Gut microbiome."

Neuroception: Coined by Dr. Stephen Porges. The nervous system's automatic, subconscious process of evaluating the environment for threat or safety signals — independent of conscious perception or rational thought. Neuroception happens faster than thought and below awareness. In panic disorder, neuroception is miscalibrated — the nervous system detects danger signals in environments that are objectively safe. Environmental design (Pillar 3) works by stacking safety signals that neuroception will register even without conscious awareness.

Neurotransmitters: Chemical messengers that nerve cells use to communicate with each other. Key neurotransmitters relevant to panic disorder include: serotonin (mood and anxiety regulation, mostly made in the gut), GABA (the main calming chemical), dopamine (motivation and reward), norepinephrine (part of fight-or-flight), and acetylcholine (activates muscles and the vagus nerve). What a person eats directly affects neurotransmitter production.

Norepinephrine (Noradrenaline): A neurotransmitter and hormone that is part of the fight-or-flight stress response — increasing heart rate, blood pressure, and alertness. Released during stress, exercise, and cold exposure. Also involved in focus and motivation. In panic disorder, the norepinephrine system is often dysregulated alongside cortisol and adrenaline.

O-P

Optic flow: The visual experience of the environment appearing to move past the eyes as the body moves forward through space. When walking outdoors, the visual field moves continuously — the ground rushes past underfoot, trees and buildings scroll past on both sides. Research shows that this continuous visual movement directly and measurably suppresses amygdala (threat alarm) activity. This benefit is absent during stationary exercise (treadmill, stationary bike, indoor exercise) where the machine moves but the visual field stays still.

Orienting response: An automatic, split-second reflex of the nervous system to any novel or unexpected stimulus — including sudden sounds. The orienting response involves a brief increase in sympathetic (stress) system activation and a momentary focusing of attention toward the stimulus. In a noisy environment, the orienting response fires repeatedly throughout the day, maintaining a chronically elevated sympathetic baseline — adding to the anxiety load without the person realizing the environment is the source.

Oxytocin: Often called "the bonding hormone" or "the safety hormone." Released in response to safe physical contact, warmth, eye contact, and feelings of social connection. Oxytocin directly activates the parasympathetic nervous system (the calm system), reduces cortisol (stress hormone), lowers blood pressure and heart rate, and produces a subjective feeling of safety and calm. Safe physical contact from a trusted person — a hug, a hand on the back — releases oxytocin in both people involved.

Parasympathetic nervous system: The "rest-and-digest" branch of the autonomic nervous system — the body's calming and recovery system. When the parasympathetic system is active, heart rate slows, breathing deepens, digestion resumes, muscles relax, and cortisol drops. The vagus nerve is the main cable of the parasympathetic system. In panic disorder, the parasympathetic system is chronically underactive — the body can't hit the brakes effectively.

Peristalsis: Pronounced peh-rih-STAL-sis. The rhythmic, wave-like muscle contractions that move food through the entire digestive tract — from the esophagus through the stomach, small intestine, and large intestine. Coordinated by the ENS (Enteric Nervous System — the gut's own brain). When the gut's nervous system is dysregulated, peristalsis becomes irregular, contributing to IBS, constipation, or diarrhea.

PM2.5 (Particulate Matter 2.5 micrometers): Fine airborne particles smaller than 2.5 micrometers in diameter — roughly 1/30th the width of a human hair. Produced by vehicle exhaust, cooking smoke, wildfire smoke, and other combustion sources. Small enough to bypass the respiratory system's normal defenses, penetrate deep into the lungs, and even enter the bloodstream. Chronic PM2.5 exposure causes body-wide inflammation, including in the brain, and is associated with increased anxiety and depression risk.

Polyvagal theory: A neuroscience framework developed by Dr. Stephen Porges that explains how the autonomic nervous system uses the vagus nerve and social and environmental signals to constantly assess whether the environment is safe, dangerous, or life-threatening. The theory describes three autonomic states: ventral vagal (safe, social, calm), sympathetic (danger, fight-or-flight), and dorsal vagal (life-threat, freeze, shut-down). Environmental design for panic disorder aims to maximize time in the ventral vagal (safe and calm) state.

PPM (Parts Per Million): A unit of measurement for concentrations of substances in air or water. 1,000 ppm CO₂ means that out of every one million air molecules, 1,000 are carbon dioxide. Outdoor air has approximately 400-420 ppm CO₂. Indoor air in closed rooms regularly reaches 1,000-2,500 ppm.

Prefrontal cortex: The front portion of the brain's outer layer — responsible for rational thinking, decision-making, emotional regulation, and dampening excessive amygdala (alarm) activity. In panic disorder, the prefrontal cortex is functionally less connected to the amygdala than in non-anxious brains — the rational brain has less ability to calm the alarm brain. Exercise, CBT, and healthy gut-brain axis function all strengthen the prefrontal cortex-amygdala connection over time.

R-S

RCT (Randomized Controlled Trial): The gold standard of scientific research. Participants are randomly assigned to different groups — some get the treatment being studied, others get a comparison treatment or no treatment (the control group). Random assignment prevents bias. When a treatment shows benefits in an RCT, that is strong evidence it actually works.

Resonant frequency breathing: Breathing at the specific rate — typically around 6 breaths per minute — at which the oscillations of the heart rate synchronize with the breath cycle, producing maximum HRV (Heart Rate Variability) amplitude. At 6 breaths per minute, the cardiac, respiratory, and blood pressure systems all oscillate in synchrony — producing what is called "physiological coherence." This rate maximizes vagal (calming nerve) tone activation per breath.

SCFAs (Short-Chain Fatty Acids): Small organic compounds produced when gut bacteria ferment (break down) dietary fiber. The three main SCFAs are butyrate, propionate, and acetate. Butyrate is the primary fuel source for the cells lining the gut wall — keeping the gut lining healthy and intact. SCFAs also directly stimulate vagal afferent neurons (the nerve cells that carry signals from the gut to the brain), improving vagal tone (the calming system's strength) and parasympathetic nervous system activity.

Serotonin: A neurotransmitter (brain chemical) that regulates mood, anxiety, sleep, appetite, and digestive movement. Often called "the feel-good chemical," though its role is more complex. About 90-95% of the body's total serotonin is manufactured in the gut — not in the brain — by specialized cells called enterochromaffin cells. Most antidepressant and anti-anxiety medications (SSRIs) work by keeping more serotonin available in the brain. Gut health directly affects serotonin production.

SSRIs (Selective Serotonin Reuptake Inhibitors): A class of medications including fluoxetine (Prozac), sertraline (Zoloft), escitalopram (Lexapro), and paroxetine (Paxil). They work by slowing the reabsorption ("reuptake") of serotonin after it is released between nerve cells, keeping more serotonin available. One of the most commonly prescribed medications for panic disorder. Because 90-95% of serotonin is made in the gut, gut health significantly affects how much serotonin is available for SSRIs to work with.

Sympathetic nervous system: The "fight-or-flight" branch of the autonomic nervous system — the body's emergency stress system. When activated, it floods the body with adrenaline and cortisol, raises heart rate, speeds breathing, tenses muscles, and shuts down digestion. In panic disorder, the sympathetic system is chronically overactivated — firing too easily and too frequently for things that are not actual emergencies.

T-Z

Theta waves (brainwave state): Electrical oscillations in the brain occurring at 4-8 Hz (cycles per second) — associated with deep relaxation, light sleep, creativity, and the drowsy state before sleep. Theta-range binaural beats (4-8 Hz) have the strongest research support for reducing anxiety and improving HRV among all binaural beat frequencies.

Vagal tone: How active, strong, and responsive the vagus nerve's calming influence is. High vagal tone means the vagus nerve is doing its job well — the body shifts smoothly and quickly from a stress state back to a calm state. Low vagal tone means the calming system is weak — the body gets stuck in high-alert mode and has trouble recovering after stress or panic. Vagal tone is measurable through HRV and is trainable through exercise, breathing, diet, and environmental changes.

Vagus nerve: The longest nerve in the body — running from the brainstem (the lower portion of the brain where it connects to the spinal cord) all the way through the throat, heart, lungs, and digestive system. It is the main cable of the parasympathetic nervous system (the rest-and-calm system). About 80% of the vagus nerve's signals travel upward — from gut to brain — meaning the gut is doing most of the "talking" in this relationship. The vagus nerve is stimulated by exercise, deep breathing, cold exposure (carefully), singing, humming, laughter, and certain foods.

VOCs (Volatile Organic Compounds): Carbon-based chemicals that evaporate easily at room temperature, releasing into the air. Many common household products release VOCs — including paints, varnishes, cleaning products, furniture adhesives, air fresheners, scented candles, and synthetic carpets. Indoor VOC concentrations are frequently 2-5 times higher than outdoor concentrations. Chronic exposure to elevated VOCs is linked to headaches, cognitive impairment, respiratory irritation, and in some studies, increased anxiety and depression.
