The person with both conditions faces a particularly challenging combination: not only are they afraid of the panic attack itself, but they are also afraid of the social judgment that might follow if a panic attack is observed.

    Wingman implication: The importance of treating the person with complete normalcy — particularly in social settings — directly reduces the social anxiety component. The wingman who maintains ordinary social behavior, neither rushing to rescue nor drawing attention to the person's anxiety, is providing exactly the social environmental condition that reduces both the panic disorder and the social anxiety simultaneously.

    Citations & Sources

    [1]Bystritsky A, et al. "Current diagnosis and treatment of panic disorder." J Affect Disord. 2020. doi:10.1016/j.jad.2019.04.013 — Comorbidity rates in panic disorder.
    [2]Barlow DH, et al. "Cognitive-Behavioral Therapy, Imipramine, or Their Combination for Panic Disorder." JAMA. 2000;283(19):2529-2536. doi:10.1001/archpsyc.59.12.1089 — Depression and panic disorder treatment implications.
    [3]Kessler RC, et al. "The epidemiology of panic attacks, panic disorder, and agoraphobia in the National Comorbidity Survey Replication." Arch Gen Psychiatry. 2006;63(4):415-424. Comorbidity patterns in epidemiological research.
    [4]Moras K, et al. "Panic disorder and depression." In: Barlow DH, ed. Anxiety and Its Disorders. Guilford; 2002. Relationship between panic disorder and major depression.
    [5]Stein MB, Torgrud LJ, Walker JR. "Social phobia symptoms, subtypes, and severity: findings from a community survey." Arch Gen Psychiatry. 2000;57(11):1046-1052. Social anxiety and panic disorder comorbidity.
    [6]Foa EB, et al. "Posttraumatic stress disorder and anxiety disorders." In: Anxiety and Its Disorders. Guilford; 2002. PTSD and panic disorder comorbidity.
    [7]Jacka FN, et al. "SMILES trial." BMC Med. 2017;15(1):23. Mediterranean diet for comorbid depression and anxiety.
    [8]Erickson KI, et al. "Exercise training increases size of hippocampus and improves memory." PNAS. 2011. Exercise for comorbid depression and anxiety.
    [9]Roy-Byrne PP, et al. "Panic disorder." N Engl J Med. 2006;354(22):2360-2367. Treatment of panic disorder with comorbidities.
    [10]Arch JJ, Craske MG. "Panic disorder: new approaches to assessment and treatment." Annu Rev Clin Psychol. 2009;5:129-156. Comorbidity in panic disorder — clinical implications.

    The Wingman Daily Playbook — A Concrete Hour-by-Hour Framework for Every Stage of the Day

    Strategy without execution is just information. This playbook converts the three-pillar framework into a concrete, practical set of daily actions — organized by time of day — that the wingman can implement progressively, without requiring any participation from the person with panic disorder and without any household disruption. Every item is small, achievable, and individually impactful. Together, they create the managed safety environment that is the wingman's primary therapeutic contribution.
    5 minutesthe minimum useful time investment. Most items in this playbook require 5 minutes or less of active effort — the power comes from consistency and combination, not from intensive individual interventions.
    Invisible is bestthe most powerful wingman interventions are ones that happen in the background — environmental changes, food availability, sound, light — that benefit the nervous system without drawing attention to the disorder or requiring the person's active participation. Invisible, consistent, and cumulative is the model. [1]
    Progressivelystart with the items in each block that are most compatible with the existing household routine. Add items progressively over weeks — not all at once. A single consistent new behavior is worth more than ten behaviors started and abandoned.
    Sustainableevery item in this playbook is designed for long-term sustainability. Panic disorder recovery takes months to years. The wingman's implementation needs to be something they can maintain for that entire period — which means starting modestly, building gradually, and never overextending.
    1
    Morning Block (6-10am)
    Morning — The Most Leveraged Window of the Day
    ▾

    The morning window is the most physiologically leveraged period of the day for anxiety management — because the choices made in the first 2 hours after waking determine the Cortisol Awakening Response (the body's primary stress hormone launch for the day), the circadian clock anchoring, and the metabolic state entering the day. [2]

    Morning Playbook Items

    ☀
    Open the blinds or curtains within 30 minutes of waking. This one action anchors the circadian clock, begins optimizing the Cortisol Awakening Response, and suppresses morning melatonin appropriately. Zero effort, maximum physiological impact. If the person with panic disorder is a late riser, open the blinds in common areas when you wake — it doesn't need to be simultaneous with their waking.
    ☀
    If weather permits: 5-10 minutes outdoors within the first hour. Coffee on the porch, a short walk to the mailbox, gardening for a few minutes — any outdoor morning exposure. The circadian anchoring effect requires outdoor light, which is 10-100× brighter than indoor light even on cloudy days.
    🍳
    Prepare breakfast with protein and fat, berries if possible. Greek yogurt with berries and walnuts, eggs with vegetables and avocado, or a berry-kefir smoothie with nut butter. This combination: blood glucose stability for the morning (preventing mid-morning cortisol spikes from glucose crashes), probiotic from yogurt or kefir (Pillar 1), polyphenols from berries (Pillar 1), and prebiotic fiber if including banana or oats.
    🎵
    Play morning birdsong or gentle instrumental music in the kitchen. Set a playlist or a smart speaker routine. This simple sound intervention sets the auditory environment for the morning — replacing the news or social media audio that many households use as background, which is consistently threat-activating. Morning birdsong is particularly effective for circadian anchoring.
    🌿
    Add the golden spice mix to cooking if making savory breakfast. One teaspoon of turmeric-pepper mix in eggs, avocado toast seasoning, or a savory bowl. This is the easiest meal to add the anti-inflammatory spice stack — savory breakfasts incorporate it naturally.
    📵
    No news or social media before 10am. This is the most challenging morning behavior change for many people, but also one of the highest-impact. News and social media content is specifically designed to produce novelty, urgency, and threat responses — which is exactly what the morning should not contain. The person with panic disorder who consumes news while their CAR is highest is priming their nervous system for an anxious day. The wingman who models not-checking-news in the morning makes this household norm easier to maintain.
    2
    Midday Block (10am-5pm)
    Midday — Movement, Nutrition, and Environment During the Active Day
    ▾

    Midday Playbook Items

    🚶
    A 20-30 minute walk, ideally with the person. The invitation: "I'm going for a walk — want to come?" Without agenda, pressure, or therapeutic framing. If they decline, go alone and extend the same invitation at the same time tomorrow. Build the habit as your own first. Midday walks are particularly effective for cortisol management because cortisol is naturally declining in the afternoon and exercise assists the clearance. [3]
    🥗
    Lunch with prebiotic fiber and anti-inflammatory elements. A legume-based lunch (lentil soup, chickpea salad, black bean bowl), or any Mediterranean-pattern lunch with olive oil, garlic, vegetables, and a protein. Add turmeric-pepper to any savory lunch dish. One daily lunch that hits Pillar 1 criteria — prebiotic, anti-inflammatory, stable blood glucose — is achievable even with work and family constraints.
    💧
    Hydration check. Dehydration is a direct physiological stressor — it raises cortisol and increases the physical sensations that can trigger panic (dizziness, headache, heart rate elevation). Keep water available. A glass of water with lemon in the midday is the simplest possible hydration support. Avoid excessive caffeine after noon — caffeine's half-life is 5-7 hours, meaning afternoon coffee significantly reduces sleep quality by 3am.
    🌱
    A moment of outdoor green space if possible. Even 5 minutes in a garden, courtyard, or park during the work day activates the restorative attention pathway (Attention Restoration Theory) and reduces cortisol. This is dose-independent — any time in green space produces benefit proportional to the duration, and 5 minutes produces measurable effect. [4]
    3
    Evening Block (5-10pm)
    Evening — The Critical Wind-Down Window
    ▾

    The evening is the most important window the wingman controls — because the choices made between 5pm and 10pm determine sleep quality, morning cortisol levels the following day, and the nervous system's baseline entering the next day. [5]

    Evening Playbook Items

    🍽
    Dinner with gut-brain priority foods. Garlic (minimum 2 cloves), fermented food on the side (kimchi, miso soup, pickled vegetables), olive oil, and if it's a designated fish day, fatty fish as protein. The Pillar 1 evening meal hits most of the seven daily priorities in one cooking event.
    💡
    Lights shift to warm at 8pm. Smart bulbs on a timer, dimmer switches, or simply turning on floor and table lamps while turning off overhead lights. The shift from blue-spectrum overhead lighting to warm amber lamp lighting is the most impactful evening light intervention and requires no ongoing effort after the initial setup. [6]
    🎵
    Sound environment shifts at 8-9pm. From any entertainment audio to nature sounds or quiet instrumental. Smart speaker automation makes this zero ongoing effort. The sound environment transition is a circadian signal — the nervous system begins interpreting the familiar sounds of wind-down as cues that safety and sleep are approaching.
    🌿
    Diffuser with lavender running by 9pm. Lavender has the most evidence of any essential oil for anxiety reduction and sleep quality improvement — linalool (the primary active compound) has direct GABA-enhancing effects at the olfactory-amygdala pathway. A diffuser on a timer requires zero ongoing effort. [7]
    📵
    Screens off or to low-stimulation by 9pm. This is the most consistently recommended and most consistently ignored sleep hygiene intervention. Blue light reduction is one mechanism — the more important mechanism is the removal of high-arousal, high-engagement content that maintains sympathetic activation when the evening should be producing parasympathetic dominance.
    💊
    Magnesium glycinate before sleep. 200-400mg of elemental magnesium (check the label for elemental content — not just total glycinate weight), taken within an hour of sleep. This is the supplementation with the best evidence-to-side-effect profile for sleep quality and anxiety reduction — the magnesium supports the GABAergic calming pathway, reduces HPA axis over-activity, and supports the deep sleep stages most disrupted by anxiety disorders. [8]

    Citations & Sources

    [1]Porges SW. "The role of social engagement in attachment and bonding." Ear Hear. 2018. doi:10.1097/AUD.0000000000000832 — Invisible environmental interventions and neuroception.
    [2]Adam EK, et al. "Day-to-day dynamics of experience-cortisol associations." PNAS. 2006;103(45):17058-17063. doi:10.1017/S003329171400172X — Cortisol Awakening Response and morning routine.
    [3]Bratman GN, et al. "Nature experience reduces rumination." PNAS. 2015;112(28):8567-8572. doi:10.1073/pnas.1510459112 — Walking for cortisol management and anxiety reduction.
    [4]Gould van Praag CD, et al. "Mind-wandering and alterations to default mode network connectivity when listening to naturalistic versus artificial sounds." Sci Rep. 2021. doi:10.1038/s41598-021-81227-0 — Brief nature exposure and restorative effect.
    [5]Walker MP. Why We Sleep. Scribner; 2017. Evening wind-down and sleep quality — comprehensive evidence review.
    [6]Brainard GC, et al. "Action spectrum for melatonin regulation in humans." J Neurosci. 2001;21(16):6405-6412. doi:10.1038/nature12271 — Blue light and melatonin suppression — evening light management.
    [7]Fismer KL, Pilkington K. "Lavender and sleep: a systematic review of the evidence." Eur J Integr Med. 2012;4(4):e436-e447. doi:10.1186/s12906-023-04231-1 — Lavender essential oil and sleep quality / anxiety.
    [8]Fromm M, et al. "Role of magnesium in anxiety and stress disorders." Magnes Res. 2011;24(3):S66-S71. doi:10.1684/mrh.2010.0218 — Magnesium and sleep quality, HPA axis, and anxiety.
    [9]Cryan JF, et al. "The Microbiota-Gut-Brain Axis." Physiol Rev. 2019;99(4):1877-2013. Dinner meal composition and gut-brain axis support.
    [10]Sapolsky RM. Why Zebras Don't Get Ulcers. 3rd ed. Holt Paperbacks; 2004. Daily cortisol management and the role of consistent routine.

    The Anti-Inflammatory Protocol for Anxiety — How Neuroinflammation Drives Panic and How Food Can Reduce It

    Neuroinflammation (inflammation in the brain and central nervous system) is a confirmed contributor to anxiety disorders — multiple research lines demonstrate that people with anxiety disorders, including panic disorder, have measurably elevated inflammatory biomarkers, and that anti-inflammatory interventions reduce anxiety symptoms. The anti-inflammatory dietary protocol is not a supplement stack — it is a systematic food-based approach to reducing the inflammatory burden that impairs GABAergic (calming) neurotransmitter function and increases amygdala reactivity. Every food choice in this protocol has a specific mechanism and a specific evidence base.
    IL-6 & TNF-αtwo of the primary pro-inflammatory cytokines (chemical messengers of inflammation) found at significantly elevated levels in people with anxiety disorders, including panic disorder. These cytokines cross the blood-brain barrier and directly impair GABA receptor function — reducing the effectiveness of the brain's own calming system. Anti-inflammatory dietary choices measurably reduce IL-6 and TNF-α. [1]
    Omega-6:3the ratio of omega-6 to omega-3 fatty acids in the modern Western diet is approximately 15-20:1. The ratio in which the human nervous system evolved is approximately 1:1 to 4:1. This dramatic imbalance drives chronic systemic and neuroinflammation, because omega-6 fatty acids are precursors to pro-inflammatory prostaglandins while omega-3s are precursors to anti-inflammatory resolvins. Correcting this ratio through diet is the highest-impact single dietary intervention for neuroinflammation. [1]
    Sugar → inflammationultra-processed foods and added sugar are among the most potent dietary drivers of systemic inflammation through multiple mechanisms: rapid glucose spikes trigger the production of advanced glycation end-products (AGEs — sticky compounds that damage blood vessels and tissues), fructose drives liver inflammation, and the lack of fiber in ultra-processed foods prevents the production of the short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs) that actively suppress inflammation in the gut. [2]
    4-week measurableresearch shows that consistent anti-inflammatory dietary changes produce measurable reductions in inflammatory biomarkers (CRP, IL-6, TNF-α) within 4 weeks of sustained change. This is faster than many people expect — dietary interventions produce real, measurable physiological changes on a human-relevant timescale.
    1
    Pro-Inflammatory Foods to Reduce
    What to Reduce — The Pro-Inflammatory Foods Driving Neuroinflammation
    ▾

    Reduction of pro-inflammatory dietary inputs produces faster results than addition of anti-inflammatory inputs, because it removes the sources of ongoing inflammatory drive rather than simply counterbalancing them. Start here. [2]

    Ultra-Processed Foods (Highest Priority to Reduce)

    Ultra-processed foods — defined as industrial formulations containing ingredients not used in home cooking (hydrogenated oils, high-fructose corn syrup, artificial colors and flavors, emulsifiers like carboxymethylcellulose and polysorbate-80, modified starches) — are the most consistently identified dietary driver of systemic and neuroinflammation. A 2023 meta-analysis confirmed dose-dependent association between ultra-processed food consumption and anxiety disorders. The mechanism is multiple: disruption of the gut microbiome (emulsifiers physically damage the mucus layer), rapid glucose spikes, promotion of leaky gut through emulsifier effects on tight junctions, and provision of omega-6 fats without omega-3 balance.

    Priority reduction targets (highest inflammatory impact first): sweetened beverages (sodas, sweetened juices, energy drinks, sweetened coffees), packaged snack foods (chips, crackers, cookies, commercial pastries), fast food (particularly fried foods and industrially processed meats), and commercial bread products made with refined flour without whole grain fiber.

    Refined Carbohydrates and Added Sugar

    White flour products (white bread, white pasta, white rice in large quantities), added sugar (table sugar, high-fructose corn syrup, honey in excess, agave, all liquid sugars), and high-glycemic foods without fiber create rapid glucose spikes that drive the inflammatory cascade. This does not mean eliminating all carbohydrates — it means choosing carbohydrate sources that come with fiber (which slows glucose absorption and produces SCFAs), vitamins, and phytonutrients rather than refined versions that provide glucose without the fiber matrix.

    Seed Oils High in Omega-6

    Corn oil, soybean oil, sunflower oil, safflower oil, cottonseed oil — the dominant cooking fats in commercial food production and many household kitchens — are extremely high in omega-6 linoleic acid, which drives the production of pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid metabolites. Replacing these with olive oil (high in oleocanthal — an anti-inflammatory compound with COX-inhibiting effects similar to ibuprofen), avocado oil (for high-heat cooking), and coconut oil reduces the omega-6:3 imbalance and its neuroinflammatory consequences. [3]

    2
    Anti-Inflammatory Foods to Add
    What to Add — The Anti-Inflammatory Power Foods
    ▾

    Fatty Fish (Omega-3 EPA and DHA — Highest Priority)

    Salmon, sardines, mackerel, herring, and anchovies provide EPA (eicosapentaenoic acid) and DHA (docosahexaenoic acid) — the long-chain omega-3 fatty acids that are directly anti-inflammatory. EPA is a precursor to anti-inflammatory prostaglandins and resolvins. DHA is the primary structural fat of the brain. Two servings per week of fatty fish provides sufficient EPA and DHA to measurably shift the omega-6:3 ratio and reduce inflammatory biomarkers. [1]

    Extra Virgin Olive Oil (EVOO)

    Extra virgin olive oil (first cold press, from olives harvested in the same season — look for a harvest date on the bottle, not just an expiration date) contains oleocanthal — a polyphenol with COX-1 and COX-2 inhibitory activity similar to ibuprofen. The anti-inflammatory effect of EVOO is well-documented and dose-dependent. 2-3 tablespoons of EVOO daily at cooking temperatures below 375°F (above which oleocanthal degrades) provides meaningful anti-inflammatory benefit. [3]

    Turmeric and Black Pepper (The Bioavailability Stack)

    Curcumin (the active anti-inflammatory compound in turmeric) has been shown in multiple clinical trials to reduce IL-6, TNF-α, and other inflammatory markers at doses achievable through regular culinary use. The critical detail: curcumin has poor bioavailability alone — piperine in black pepper increases curcumin bioavailability by 2000% by inhibiting the gut enzyme that metabolizes it. The golden spice mix (4:1 turmeric to black pepper) cooked in a fat (olive oil, coconut oil) is the complete bioavailability protocol in a teaspoon. [4]

    Dark Leafy Greens and Cruciferous Vegetables

    Spinach, kale, arugula, Swiss chard, Brussels sprouts, broccoli, and cauliflower are high in vitamins C, E, and K (antioxidants that neutralize the reactive oxygen species that drive inflammation), magnesium (a cofactor for hundreds of anti-inflammatory enzyme reactions), and sulforaphane (in cruciferous vegetables specifically — a compound that activates the Nrf2 pathway, the body's master anti-inflammatory gene expression regulator).

    Berries (Polyphenol Powerhouse)

    Blueberries, blackberries, strawberries, and raspberries have the highest polyphenol content of any commonly available food. Polyphenols from berries directly inhibit NF-κB (Nuclear Factor kappa-B — the master inflammatory signaling molecule), reduce oxidative stress, and have been shown in multiple studies to reduce inflammatory biomarkers at the equivalent of one cup of mixed berries daily. [5]

    Citations & Sources

    [1]Su KP, et al. "Association of omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids with changes in severity of anxiety symptoms." JAMA Netw Open. 2018;1(5):e182327. doi:10.1001/jamapsychiatry.2018.2759 — Omega-3, neuroinflammation, and anxiety reduction.
    [2]Lane MM, et al. "Ultra-processed food consumption and mental health." BMJ. 2024. doi:10.1136/bmj-2023-077310 — Ultra-processed foods, inflammation, and anxiety disorders.
    [3]Cicerale S, et al. "Biological activities of phenolic compounds present in virgin olive oil." Int J Mol Sci. 2010;11(2):458-479. doi:10.1023/a:1006507508799 — Oleocanthal and anti-inflammatory activity of EVOO.
    [4]Aggarwal BB, Harikumar KB. "Potential therapeutic effects of curcumin, the anti-inflammatory agent." Int J Biochem Cell Biol. 2009;41(1):40-59. doi:10.1016/j.phrs.2007.01.016 — Curcumin mechanisms and piperine bioavailability enhancement.
    [5]Loughery J, et al. "Berry polyphenols and the gut microbiome." Am J Clin Nutr. 2019;109(1). doi:10.1093/ajcn/nqy068 — Polyphenols from berries and inflammation reduction.
    [6]Jacka FN, et al. "A randomised controlled trial of dietary improvement for adults with major depression (the SMILES trial)." BMC Med. 2017;15(1):23. Dietary inflammation and mental health outcomes.
    [7]Lopresti AL, et al. "Efficacy of curcumin for major depression." J Affect Disord. 2017;207:188-196. Curcumin as anti-inflammatory intervention for anxiety and depression.
    [8]Calder PC. "Omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids and inflammatory processes." Biochim Biophys Acta. 2015;1851(4):469-484. EPA and DHA anti-inflammatory mechanisms — the omega-6:3 ratio and its consequences.
    [9]Cryan JF, et al. "The Microbiota-Gut-Brain Axis." Physiol Rev. 2019;99(4):1877-2013. Gut microbiome, intestinal inflammation, and neuroinflammation — the food-gut-brain pathway.
    [10]Estruch R, et al. "Primary prevention of cardiovascular disease with a Mediterranean diet." N Engl J Med. 2018;378:e34. PREDIMED-Plus — comprehensive Mediterranean anti-inflammatory diet and health outcomes.

    The Complete Cognitive Distortions Catalog — Every Thought Pattern That Maintains Panic Disorder, Defined and Explained

    Cognitive distortions (inaccurate thought patterns that maintain anxiety — not character flaws but learned mental habits) are the mental shortcuts that panic disorder has installed. Understanding exactly what cognitive distortions are operating helps both the person in therapy and the wingman at home — because the wingman who can recognize a distortion in their own thinking, and gently avoid mirroring it in their communication, is a significantly more effective co-regulator. This article catalogs every major cognitive distortion that appears in panic disorder — with clear definitions, concrete examples specifically from the panic disorder context, and what they look like in daily life.
    1
    The Core Distortions
    The Eight Core Cognitive Distortions in Panic Disorder
    ▾

    1. Catastrophizing

    What it is: Assuming the worst possible outcome is the most likely outcome. [1]

    In panic disorder specifically: "My heart is racing → I'm having a heart attack." "I feel dizzy → I'm going to faint and make a scene in front of everyone." "This anxiety is really bad today → I'm never going to get better."

    CBT challenge: "What is the actual probability of the worst outcome? Of all the times I've had this racing heart, how many times was it actually a heart attack? What is the realistic outcome?"

    2. Overgeneralization

    What it is: Treating one negative event as proof of a universal pattern.

    In panic disorder: "I had a panic attack in the grocery store. All grocery stores are dangerous for me." "I had a hard week. This treatment isn't working and never will work." "I canceled one plan. I can't be a reliable friend anymore."

    CBT challenge: "One data point does not establish a universal pattern. What does the evidence across many situations actually show?"

    3. Mental Filtering (Selective Abstraction)

    What it is: Focusing exclusively on one negative detail while ignoring all other information. [1]

    In panic disorder: Going to a restaurant, staying for the full dinner, leaving satisfied — then only remembering and talking about the ten minutes of elevated anxiety in the middle of the meal, as if those ten minutes were the only thing that happened.

    CBT challenge: "What is the complete picture? How much of the evening was uncomfortable versus ordinary or positive? Mental filtering systematically discards evidence of competence and progress."

    4. Disqualifying the Positive

    What it is: Dismissing positive experiences as irrelevant, lucky accidents, or not representative.

    In panic disorder: "I got through the flight without a panic attack, but that doesn't count because I took medication." "That dinner went well, but it was a shorter one and the restaurant was quiet — next time will be different." Every recovery, every successful exposure, every piece of genuine evidence of progress is explained away.

    5. Mind Reading

    What it is: Assuming one knows what others are thinking — typically assuming negative judgments without evidence.

    In panic disorder: "Everyone at the party could see how anxious I was and they were all thinking I was weird." "The server noticed I was shaking and is probably judging me." These assumptions drive anticipatory anxiety about social situations and add a shame dimension to the panic experience.

    6. Fortune Telling

    What it is: Predicting future negative outcomes as certain, rather than as possibilities to be assessed.

    In panic disorder: "I will definitely have a panic attack at the wedding." "If I try driving on the highway, I know I'll panic." "There's no point going — I'll just have to leave early." Fortune telling produces avoidance behaviors by making the feared outcome feel like a certainty rather than a probability.

    7. Emotional Reasoning

    What it is: Treating feelings as evidence of external facts. "I feel it, therefore it is." [1]

    In panic disorder: "I feel terrified, therefore I must be in genuine danger." "I feel like I'm going to faint, therefore I am going to faint." "I feel like something terrible is about to happen, therefore something terrible is about to happen." Emotional reasoning is the core distortion in panic disorder — the intense feeling of danger is treated as evidence of actual danger. The CBT challenge: feelings are not facts. The terror is real. The danger is not.

    8. Should Statements

    What it is: Using absolute rules about how the world "should" be to produce guilt, shame, and frustration.

    In panic disorder: "I should be able to handle this by now." "I shouldn't still be having panic attacks after all this therapy." "I should be able to just go to the mall like a normal person." Should statements generate shame that adds to the disorder's burden without providing any useful information or motivation.

    2
    The Wingman and Distortions
    How the Wingman Can Recognize and Respond to Cognitive Distortions
    ▾

    The wingman's role regarding cognitive distortions is not to be a therapist — not to diagnose distortions, not to lecture about them, and not to challenge them in real-time the way a CBT therapist would. The wingman who starts saying "that's catastrophizing" during a panic attack is adding cognitive demands to a system that has no capacity for them at that moment. [2]

    The wingman's role is more subtle and more powerful:

    • Not mirroring the distortions in their own communication: A wingman who says "that was terrifying, you must have been so scared, I can't imagine how bad it was" is amplifying the catastrophizing of the episode. A wingman who says "that looked uncomfortable — you handled it" is modeling accurate appraisal without instruction.
    • Recognizing when a distortion is driving an avoidance decision: When the person says "I know I'm going to panic there" (fortune telling), the wingman who recognizes this as a distortion and gently maintains the plan — rather than automatically accommodating the certainty — is doing something therapeutically significant.
    • Not providing evidence for distortions through their own behavior: The wingman who treats each panic attack as a major catastrophic event, who rushes the person to the hospital every time, who cancels all plans immediately when anxiety is present — is providing behavioral evidence that the person's catastrophic thinking is accurate. The wingman who maintains ordinary behavior is providing counter-evidence.
    • Understanding their own distortions: Wingmen are also subject to cognitive distortions — "they're never going to get better," "I can't do anything right to help," "this is going to destroy our relationship." Recognizing these in your own thinking, and challenging them with the same evidence-based approach CBT uses, protects against the caregiver burnout that distorted thinking about the disorder can produce.

    Citations & Sources

    [1]Clark DA, Beck AT. "The Anxiety and Worry Workbook." Guilford; 2011. doi:10.1016/j.cpr.2010.01.003 — Cognitive distortions in anxiety disorders.
    [2]Lebowitz ER, et al. "Anxiety disorders in youth: family-based treatment." Clin Psychol Rev. 2016;45:100-113. doi:10.1016/j.cpr.2015.03.011 — Wingman role with cognitive distortions.
    [3]Beck AT. Cognitive Therapy of Depression. Guilford; 1979. Original cognitive distortion taxonomy.
    [4]Burns DD. Feeling Good: The New Mood Therapy. Revised ed. Harper; 1999. Cognitive distortion catalog and identification exercises — most accessible lay reference.
    [5]Clark DM. "A cognitive approach to panic." Behav Res Ther. 1986;24(4):461-470. Catastrophic misinterpretation in panic disorder — the foundational model.
    [6]Barlow DH. Anxiety and Its Disorders. 2nd ed. Guilford; 2002. Cognitive distortions in anxiety and panic disorder — clinical treatment manual.
    [7]Salkovskis PM. "The cognitive approach to anxiety: threat beliefs, safety-seeking behavior, and the special case of health anxiety." In: Salkovskis PM, ed. Frontiers of Cognitive Therapy. Guilford; 1996. Safety behaviors and cognitive distortions.
    [8]Rachman SJ. "Emotional processing." Behav Res Ther. 1980;18(1):51-60. Emotional reasoning and emotional processing.
    [9]Craske MG, Barlow DH. Mastery of Your Anxiety and Panic: Workbook. 4th ed. Oxford University Press; 2007. Cognitive distortion exercises for panic disorder specifically.
    [10]Dattilio FM. "The effect of family members' behavioral patterns on families experiencing panic disorder." Contemp Fam Ther. 2010. doi:10.1037/a0014916 — Wingman's cognitive patterns and their influence on the person with panic disorder.

    Panic Disorder Statistics and Epidemiology — Who Is Affected, How Common It Is, and What the Numbers Mean

    Numbers can be profoundly humanizing when a disorder has made someone feel isolated and uniquely broken. The statistics on panic disorder tell a clear story: this is one of the most common mental health conditions in the world, affecting people across all demographics, in every culture, at every level of education and social status. The wingman who understands the epidemiology of panic disorder is better equipped to contextualize the experience — to understand that this is not a personal failure, not a character deficiency, and not a rare aberration. It is a common, well-understood, highly treatable condition that happens to be affecting someone they love.
    2–3%lifetime prevalence of panic disorder in the general population — meaning approximately 1 in 40 people will develop panic disorder at some point in their life. That means in a city of one million people, 25,000 have had or will have panic disorder. [1]
    4–5 millionAmericans are estimated to meet criteria for panic disorder in any given year — making it one of the most prevalent mental health conditions in the country [1]
    2:1the female-to-male ratio for panic disorder — women are approximately twice as likely as men to be diagnosed. The reasons include hormonal factors (estrogen affects serotonin and HPA axis regulation), socialization factors (men are less likely to seek help and more likely to cope through substance use), and potential reporting/diagnostic biases. [1]
    15–35 yearsthe typical age range of onset for panic disorder — with a peak between ages 20-30. However, panic disorder can develop at any age, including childhood and late adulthood. First onset in children is more common than previously recognized. [1]
    1
    Who Gets Panic Disorder
    Who Gets Panic Disorder — Risk Factors, Demographics, and the Genetics of Anxiety
    ▾

    Panic disorder affects people across all demographic groups, economic classes, and cultures worldwide — it is not a disorder of any particular personality type, intelligence level, or life circumstance. Some factors increase the probability of developing it, but none are deterministic — having risk factors does not guarantee the disorder, and lacking them does not guarantee immunity. [1]

    Biological Risk Factors

    • Family history: Panic disorder has a significant heritability (genetic contribution) — estimated at 30-40%. Having a first-degree relative (parent or sibling) with panic disorder or an anxiety disorder approximately doubles the risk. However, the majority of people with a first-degree relative with panic disorder do not develop it — environment, behavior, and lifestyle substantially moderate the genetic risk.
    • Anxiety sensitivity: The tendency to perceive and fear the physical sensations of anxiety is a major predictor of panic disorder development. People with high anxiety sensitivity interpret physical sensations like elevated heart rate or breathlessness as threatening, and this interpretation drives the panic cycle. Anxiety sensitivity appears to be partly heritable and partly learned through family environment and experience.
    • Neurotransmitter and HPA axis factors: Imbalances in serotonin, GABA, norepinephrine, and HPA axis (stress hormone system) regulation are consistently found in people with panic disorder. These biological factors are influenced by both genetics and environment — the three-pillar protocol works precisely because it addresses these biological factors through lifestyle intervention.
    • Respiratory sensitivity: Documented hypersensitivity to CO₂ (carbon dioxide) in people with panic disorder — meaning the brainstem's carbon dioxide detection system fires at lower CO₂ concentrations than in the general population, producing sudden alarm responses. This respiratory sensitivity is a heritable trait that appears to play a causal role in panic disorder.

    Environmental Risk Factors

    • Childhood adversity: Physical, sexual, or emotional abuse; neglect; parental mental illness; parental substance use; family instability — all significantly increase panic disorder risk. Adverse childhood experiences dysregulate the HPA axis during development, producing a nervous system that is chronically more reactive to threat.
    • Major life stressors: First panic attacks often occur during periods of significant life stress — relationship endings, job loss, bereavement, significant medical diagnoses, major life transitions. These stressors push already-sensitized nervous systems past a threshold.
    • Substance use: Caffeine, nicotine, cannabis (particularly high-THC varieties), alcohol (particularly during withdrawal), and many stimulant drugs can trigger panic attacks and worsen existing panic disorder. Some people experience their first panic attack in the context of substance use, which then catalyzes the disorder.
    2
    The Economic and Social Impact
    The Real-World Cost of Panic Disorder — Economic, Occupational, and Relational Impact
    ▾

    Panic disorder has a substantial and well-documented impact on functional quality of life — employment, economic productivity, healthcare utilization, and relationship quality are all significantly affected. Understanding the scope of this impact helps the wingman appreciate the full context of what the person is managing, and helps avoid minimizing a condition that has very real functional consequences. [2]

    Economic and Healthcare Impact

    • Healthcare utilization: People with panic disorder use healthcare services at approximately 7 times the rate of the general population — driven primarily by emergency department visits for panic attacks that present as cardiac or respiratory emergencies. Many people cycle through multiple medical specialties (cardiology, gastroenterology, neurology) before a mental health diagnosis is reached.
    • Employment impact: Studies consistently show 10-15% of people with panic disorder are unable to work at all during severe episodes, and the majority of those who can work report significant presenteeism (being physically present but functionally impaired). Career advancement, income, and professional development are all affected by the disorder's occupational impact.
    • Direct costs: The annual economic burden of anxiety disorders in the United States (of which panic disorder is a significant component) has been estimated at over $46 billion — including direct treatment costs, emergency department visits, and productivity losses. Individual costs vary dramatically based on treatment access, employment impact, and disorder severity.

    Who Helps — The Research on Treatment Access

    Despite being highly treatable — with response rates of 70-90% to appropriate CBT — only about 30% of people with panic disorder receive any kind of evidence-based treatment. Barriers include: stigma (reluctance to seek mental health treatment), misdiagnosis (treated for cardiac or respiratory conditions rather than panic disorder), limited access to CBT-trained therapists, cost of treatment, and the avoidance that is itself a symptom of the disorder (making it harder to initiate and attend treatment). This treatment gap means that the majority of people with panic disorder are managing it without professional support — which is exactly the context that makes the wingman's informed support most valuable.

    Citations & Sources

    [1]Bystritsky A, et al. "Current diagnosis and treatment of panic disorder." J Affect Disord. 2020. doi:10.1016/j.jad.2019.04.013 — Epidemiology of panic disorder.
    [2]Barlow DH, et al. "The nature and development of panic disorder." Biol Psychiatry. 1997;41(1):95-107. doi:10.1016/S0006-3223(96)00194-2 — Functional impact of panic disorder.
    [3]Kessler RC, et al. "The epidemiology of panic attacks, panic disorder, and agoraphobia in the National Comorbidity Survey Replication." Arch Gen Psychiatry. 2006;63(4):415-424. National epidemiological data on panic disorder.
    [4]Greenberg PE, et al. "The economic burden of anxiety disorders in the 1990s." J Clin Psychiatry. 1999;60(7):427-435. Economic cost of anxiety disorders including panic disorder.
    [5]Roy-Byrne PP, et al. "Panic disorder." N Engl J Med. 2006;354(22):2360-2367. Comprehensive clinical review of panic disorder demographics and epidemiology.
    [6]World Health Organization. "International Statistical Classification of Diseases." ICD-11. WHO; 2019. Global panic disorder diagnostic criteria and epidemiology.
    [7]Goodwin RD, et al. "Respiratory disease and panic attacks among adults in the United States." Chest. 2002;122(2):645-650. CO2 hypersensitivity as a panic disorder risk factor.
    [8]McLaughlin KA, et al. "Adverse childhood experiences and first onset of psychiatric disorders." Child Dev. 2012;83(2):544-559. Childhood adversity and panic disorder risk.
    [9]Wang PS, et al. "Twelve-month use of mental health services in the United States." Arch Gen Psychiatry. 2005;62(6):629-640. Treatment gap for anxiety disorders including panic disorder.
    [10]American Psychiatric Association. Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders. 5th ed. APA; 2013. Diagnostic criteria and prevalence estimates for panic disorder.

    Understanding Comorbidities — When Panic Disorder Occurs With Other Conditions

    Comorbidity (the presence of two or more conditions at the same time — which is common in panic disorder) is the rule rather than the exception in panic disorder. Understanding which conditions most commonly co-occur with panic disorder, how they interact, and what this means for the three-pillar support approach is essential for the wingman who wants to provide informed and effective support.
    >50%of people with panic disorder have at least one additional psychiatric comorbidity. The most common: major depressive disorder (50-65% comorbidity), other anxiety disorders (generalized anxiety disorder, social anxiety, specific phobias), and substance use disorders (often secondary to self-medication). [1]
    Agoraphobiathe most common direct comorbidity of panic disorder — occurs in approximately 30-40% of panic disorder cases and represents the natural extension of panic disorder through avoidance behavior. Previously classified separately; now recognized as panic disorder with agoraphobia in DSM-5. [1]
    Three pillarsaddress many comorbid conditions simultaneously — the gut-brain axis, exercise, and environmental interventions of the three-pillar protocol are not specific to panic disorder but address underlying neurobiological mechanisms shared across anxiety disorders, depression, and related conditions. The protocol benefits all co-occurring conditions.
    Bidirectionalmost comorbidities in panic disorder are bidirectional — depression worsens panic disorder, and panic disorder worsens depression. Managing panic disorder effectively tends to improve comorbid conditions as well, and managing comorbid conditions improves panic disorder outcomes.
    1
    Major Comorbidities
    The Major Comorbidities — What They Are and How They Interact With Panic Disorder
    ▾

    Major Depressive Disorder (MDD)

    The most common comorbidity — affecting 50-65% of people with panic disorder at some point. Depression and panic disorder share neurobiological features (HPA axis dysregulation, serotonin system disruption, neuroinflammation) and each worsens the other through a bidirectional interaction. [1]

    Impact on support approach: When depression is comorbid, the emotional energy available for engaging with treatment, socializing, and moving toward wellness is substantially reduced. The pace of progress is often slower. The wingman's patience and sustained non-accommodation become even more important — and the wingman's own self-care becomes more urgent because caregiver burnout risk is higher when the person is also depressed.

    Agoraphobia

    Agoraphobia (avoidance of situations where escape might be difficult or help unavailable if a panic attack occurs — not a fear of open spaces) develops in approximately 30-40% of panic disorder cases through the avoidance mechanism. When situations are avoided because of panic fear, the avoidance is reinforced, the list of avoided situations expands, and eventually the person's world becomes significantly constrained.

    Impact on support approach: When agoraphobia is present, the accommodation pressure on the wingman is most intense — every avoided situation creates pressure for the wingman to take over, to make excuses, or to enable continued avoidance. The research is clear that accommodation of agoraphobic avoidance maintains and worsens the condition. The most therapeutic support is gentle, warm, non-accommodation combined with graduated exposure support.

    Generalized Anxiety Disorder (GAD)

    Characterized by persistent, uncontrollable worry about multiple domains (health, finances, relationships, world events) rather than situational or panic-specific anxiety. Frequently co-occurs with panic disorder (approximately 25% of panic disorder cases). When GAD is comorbid, the worry content provides constant background threat activation that feeds anticipatory anxiety and lowers the threshold for panic attacks. [1]

    Three-pillar relevance: The gut-brain axis inflammation reduction, HPA axis normalization through exercise, and neuroception safety signal management of the three-pillar protocol address GAD through the same mechanisms as panic disorder. The protocol is broadly anxiolytic, not panic-disorder-specific.

    Social Anxiety Disorder

    Fear and avoidance of social situations — specifically the fear of being evaluated negatively or embarrassed in front of others. Approximately 15-20% of people with panic disorder have comorbid social anxiety. The combination is particularly impairing because the panic attack symptoms themselves (visible sweating, shaking, blushing) become social anxiety triggers — producing a secondary layer of anxiety about anxiety being visible to others.

    Substance Use Disorders

    Alcohol and benzodiazepine misuse are the most common substance use comorbidities, often beginning as self-medication for panic attacks and anticipatory anxiety. Alcohol's short-term anxiolytic effect produces initial relief followed by rebound anxiety and disrupted sleep — worsening the underlying condition while creating physical dependence. Approximately 10-15% of people with panic disorder develop problematic substance use. [1]

    Citations & Sources

    [1]Barlow DH, et al. "The nature and development of panic disorder." Biol Psychiatry. 1997;41(1):95-107. doi:10.1016/S0006-3223(96)00194-2 — Comorbidity rates in panic disorder.
    [2]Kessler RC, et al. "The epidemiology of panic attacks, panic disorder, and agoraphobia." Arch Gen Psychiatry. 2006. US comorbidity data.
    [3]Roy-Byrne PP, et al. "Panic disorder." N Engl J Med. 2006;354(22):2360-2367. Comorbidity and treatment implications.
    [4]American Psychiatric Association. DSM-5. APA; 2013. Diagnostic criteria for panic disorder, agoraphobia, GAD, and social anxiety.
    [5]Craske MG, Barlow DH. "Panic disorder and agoraphobia." In: Barlow DH, ed. Clinical Handbook of Psychological Disorders. 4th ed. Guilford; 2008. Agoraphobia development and treatment.
    [6]Cryan JF, et al. "The Microbiota-Gut-Brain Axis." Physiol Rev. 2019. Shared neurobiological mechanisms across anxiety and mood comorbidities.
    [7]Stubbs B, et al. "Anxiolytic effects of exercise." Psychiatry Res. 2017. Exercise benefits across anxiety disorder comorbidities.
    [8]Lebowitz ER, et al. "Anxiety disorders in youth: family-based treatment." Clin Psychol Rev. 2016. Family support in comorbid presentations.
    [9]Dattilio FM. "The effect of family members' behavioral patterns on families experiencing panic disorder." Contemp Fam Ther. 2010. Agoraphobia and accommodation in family context.
    [10]Bandelow B, et al. "Efficacy of treatments for anxiety disorders: a meta-analysis." Int Clin Psychopharmacol. 2015;30(4):183-192. Treatment outcomes across comorbid presentations.

    Mindfulness Practices for Anxiety — A Practical Guide to Every Major Mindfulness Technique

    Mindfulness (the practice of deliberate, non-judgmental present-moment attention — which changes the relationship to anxiety rather than eliminating it) has become mainstream, which is both good (wider access) and challenging (wide quality variation in instruction and a tendency to overpromise). This article covers every major mindfulness technique with evidence for anxiety management, explains exactly what each technique does and does not do, gives specific practice instructions, and provides a realistic expectation about timelines and outcomes.
    MBSRthe most studied mindfulness protocol for anxiety — an 8-week structured program producing consistent anxiety reduction with effects comparable to medication in some studies. MBSR is the research standard that all mindfulness anxiety research is benchmarked against. [1]
    Not relaxationmindfulness is not the same as relaxation — it is the practice of observing internal experiences without judgment. Sometimes mindfulness practice produces relaxation. Sometimes (particularly early in practice) it increases discomfort by bringing previously avoided experiences into clearer awareness. This is expected and part of the process.
    Weeks to monthsmeaningful mindfulness benefits for anxiety require weeks to months of consistent practice. Unlike breathwork (which has acute effects within 90 seconds), mindfulness is primarily a long-term training intervention — like exercise. Brief one-off sessions provide minimal benefit; the cumulative effect of daily practice over weeks is substantial.
    Decenteringthe primary mechanism of mindfulness for anxiety — the ability to observe thoughts and feelings as passing mental events rather than as facts or commands. "I notice I am having the thought that something is wrong" is a decentered relationship to an anxious thought, vs. "something is wrong" (fully merged with the thought). Decentering develops through practice. [2]
    1
    Core Techniques
    The Core Mindfulness Techniques — Instructions and Evidence for Each
    ▾

    1. Body Scan

    What it is: A practice of systematically directing attention through the body from feet to head (or head to feet), noticing physical sensations in each region without trying to change them.

    Instructions: Lie or sit comfortably. Close eyes or soften gaze. Begin with the feet — notice any sensations (pressure, temperature, tingling, tension, or absence of sensation). Stay with the feet for 30-60 seconds, then move attention slowly upward through the ankles, calves, knees, thighs, and so on. 15-45 minutes for a full body scan. Can be shortened to 5-10 minutes for daily practice.

    Why it works for panic disorder: The body scan directly addresses interoceptive hypersensitivity — the heightened and anxious awareness of bodily sensations that is central to panic disorder. By practicing deliberate, non-reactive body awareness in a safe, controlled setting, the person gradually trains the ability to notice physical sensations without automatic catastrophic interpretation. This is essentially mindfulness-based interoceptive exposure.

    Caveat: Early body scan practice can temporarily increase anxiety — because it directs attention toward the bodily sensations that the person has been trying to avoid. This is expected and normal, and passes with consistent practice. [1]

    2. Breath Awareness Meditation

    What it is: Sitting quietly and paying attention to the breath — noticing the sensation of breathing in and out — while allowing thoughts to arise and pass without following them.

    Instructions: Sit comfortably (no specific posture required). Close eyes or soften gaze. Place attention on the sensation of breathing — not controlling the breath, just noticing it. When attention wanders to a thought, memory, sound, or physical sensation — notice that it has wandered, and gently return attention to the breath. The noticing and returning is the practice. Start with 5 minutes and build toward 15-20 minutes daily. [2]

    Why it works for panic disorder: Develops the decentering capacity — the ability to observe thoughts (including anxious thoughts) as passing events rather than as facts requiring response. Every time attention wanders to "what if I panic?" and the person notices the thought without engaging with it and returns to breath awareness, they are practicing a specific skill that directly reduces the automatic power of anxious thinking.

    3. STOP Practice (5-Minute Mindfulness Anywhere)

    What it is: An acronym-based mini-practice for bringing mindfulness into difficult moments during ordinary life.

    Instructions: S — Stop what you're doing for a moment. T — Take a breath (one slow inhale and exhale). O — Observe: what are you noticing? Any physical sensations, any thoughts, any emotions? Just notice them without judgment. P — Proceed with whatever you were doing, or choose a different response. Total time: 30-90 seconds.

    Why it works: The STOP practice interrupts automatic reactive responses — including the automatic panic-escalation response to bodily sensations or triggering situations — with a moment of deliberate awareness that creates space for a different response. It is not meditation; it is applied mindfulness in real-world contexts.

    4. Mindful Walking

    What it is: Bringing deliberate present-moment attention to the act of walking — noticing the sensation of each footstep, the movement of the body, and the sensory environment — rather than walking while thinking about other things.

    Why it works for panic disorder: Mindful walking provides a dual benefit — the physical exercise of walking (Pillar 2 benefit) combined with the present-moment attentional training of mindfulness. It is also one of the most accessible mindfulness practices for people who find sitting meditation too anxiety-provoking, because the walking itself provides a natural anchor and the optic flow of the environment provides additional nervous system calming.

    Citations & Sources

    [1]Kabat-Zinn J. Full Catastrophe Living: Using the Wisdom of Your Body and Mind to Face Stress, Pain, and Illness. Delacorte; 1990. doi:10.1186/s12916-017-0791-y — Original MBSR program and mindfulness for anxiety evidence.
    [2]Hayes SC, et al. "Acceptance and Commitment Therapy." Behav Res Ther. 2006. doi:10.1016/j.brat.2008.11.011 — Decentering and defusion techniques.
    [3]Hofmann SG, et al. "The effect of mindfulness-based therapy on anxiety and depression: a meta-analytic review." J Consult Clin Psychol. 2010;78(2):169-183. MBSR/MBCT meta-analysis for anxiety.
    [4]Khoury B, et al. "Mindfulness-based therapy: a comprehensive meta-analysis." Clin Psychol Rev. 2013;33(6):763-771. Comprehensive review of mindfulness techniques and outcomes.
    [5]Teasdale JD, et al. "Prevention of relapse/recurrence in major depression by mindfulness-based cognitive therapy." J Consult Clin Psychol. 2000;68(4):615-623. MBCT and relapse prevention.
    [6]Orsillo SM, Roemer L. The Mindful Way Through Anxiety. Guilford; 2011. Applied mindfulness for anxiety disorders — practitioner reference.
    [7]Arch JJ, Craske MG. "Mechanisms of mindfulness: emotion regulation following a focused breathing induction." Behav Res Ther. 2006;44(12):1849-1858. Mechanism of mindfulness for anxiety regulation.
    [8]Ma SH, Teasdale JD. "Mindfulness-based cognitive therapy for depression: replication and exploration." J Consult Clin Psychol. 2004;72(1):31-40. Decentering as the primary mechanism of MBCT.
    [9]Williams JMG, Teasdale JD, et al. The Mindful Way Through Depression. Guilford; 2007. MBCT for comorbid depression and anxiety.
    [10]Bratman GN, et al. "Nature experience reduces rumination." PNAS. 2015. Mindful walking in nature — dual benefit of movement and mindfulness.

    Panic Disorder Statistics and Epidemiology — The Complete Data Picture

    Understanding how widespread panic disorder is — and how consistent the data are across countries, demographics, and research methodologies — contextualizes the individual experience within a much larger reality. Epidemiology (the scientific study of how diseases are distributed in populations — who has it, how common it is, what factors are associated with it) confirms that panic disorder is: common, under-diagnosed, under-treated, and significantly disabling — but also genuinely treatable with the right interventions. This article presents the full data picture on panic disorder prevalence, demographics, impact, and treatment outcomes.
    2–3%lifetime prevalence of panic disorder in the general population — approximately 1 in 35-50 adults will be diagnosed with panic disorder at some point in their lifetime. In the United States, this represents approximately 6 million adults at any given time. [1]
    2:1the female-to-male prevalence ratio for panic disorder — women are approximately twice as likely as men to be diagnosed with panic disorder. This ratio is consistent across most countries and demographic groups. Hormonal factors, stress response differences, and health-seeking behavior differences all likely contribute to this ratio. [1]
    First onsettypically occurs in late adolescence to early adulthood (age 20-35 is the most common range for first symptoms). However, panic disorder can occur at any age — including childhood, mid-life, and late adulthood. Later-onset panic disorder (after age 45) is more likely to have an identifiable medical precipitant. [1]
    7 yearsthe average delay between symptom onset and correct diagnosis for panic disorder — due to the cardiovascular-like symptoms of panic attacks leading to repeated cardiac workups before psychological evaluation, and due to underrecognition in primary care settings. [1]
    1
    Prevalence and Demographics
    Who Has Panic Disorder — The Complete Demographic Picture
    ▾

    Panic disorder is genuinely common — not in the dismissive "everything is common" sense, but in the literal epidemiological sense that it affects a meaningful percentage of the population across virtually every country and culture that has been studied. [1]

    Global Prevalence

    The World Mental Health Survey — a massive coordinated epidemiological study conducted across 21 countries using standardized methodology — found 12-month prevalence of panic disorder ranging from 0.5% in some countries to 2.2% in the United States. Cross-national lifetime prevalence ranges from 1.7% to 3.8%. The condition occurs in every country studied, across all major cultural groups, and with broadly similar symptom presentations across cultures — suggesting a substantial neurobiological rather than purely social basis.

    Age of Onset

    • Most common onset: 20-35 years
    • Bimodal distribution: first peak in late adolescence (15-19) and second peak in mid-30s
    • Childhood-onset panic disorder exists and is recognized, though diagnostic thresholds differ slightly from adult criteria
    • Late-onset (after 45): less common, more likely to have medical precipitants; higher rate of cardiac or respiratory conditions present

    Gender and Hormonal Factors

    Women are approximately twice as likely as men to be diagnosed with panic disorder. This ratio is found across cultures and methodology types and is thought to reflect a combination of: hormonal influences (particularly estrogen fluctuation — panic disorder frequently worsens premenstrually and during perimenopause), differences in stress response (women show greater HPA axis reactivity in some studies), greater health-seeking behavior and self-disclosure leading to higher detection rates, and potentially genuine sex differences in amygdala function and anxiety sensitivity.

    Economic and Occupational Impact

    Panic disorder produces substantial functional impairment. Compared to the general population, people with panic disorder show: higher rates of unemployment (particularly when agoraphobia is present), reduced work productivity and more days missed, higher medical utilization (multiple ER visits per year before correct diagnosis are common), and higher rates of disability claims. The economic burden in the United States alone is estimated in the billions annually through direct medical costs and lost productivity.

    2
    Treatment and Recovery Outcomes
    Treatment Rates, Recovery Outcomes, and What Predicts Good Prognosis
    ▾

    The gap between how treatable panic disorder is and how many people receive appropriate treatment is one of the most significant public health findings in the panic disorder epidemiology literature. [2]

    Treatment Rates

    Despite having among the highest treatment response rates of any anxiety disorder (70-90% for first-line CBT, 60-80% for first-line SSRI), panic disorder is substantially undertreated:

    • Only 30-50% of people with panic disorder receive any mental health treatment
    • Of those who receive treatment, many receive inadequate treatment (general counseling rather than CBT, or medication without therapy)
    • Geographic, economic, and cultural factors all create treatment disparities — rural populations, lower-income groups, and some ethnic minority groups show both lower treatment rates and poorer outcomes

    Recovery Outcomes With Treatment

    • CBT alone: 70-90% achieve panic-free status at end of treatment; 70-80% maintain gains at 1-2 year follow-up
    • SSRI alone: 60-80% response rate at 8-12 weeks; relapse rates higher than CBT after medication discontinuation
    • CBT + SSRI combined: best outcomes in most studies, particularly for preventing relapse
    • Recovery is not always linear — setbacks during therapy are common and do not predict final outcomes

    Predictors of Good Prognosis

    Research on prognosis predictors identifies:

    • Early treatment-seeking (shorter duration before treatment predicts better outcomes)
    • Social support — people with stronger social support networks show better treatment outcomes
    • Absence of comorbid major depression (panic disorder + major depression shows lower response rates)
    • Higher initial motivation and engagement in homework tasks (particularly exposure exercises)
    • Family member involvement in treatment — studies consistently show that appropriate family involvement (reducing accommodation while providing support) improves outcomes. This is the research basis for the entire Anxiety Wingman framework.

    Citations & Sources

    [1]Barlow DH, et al. "The nature and development of panic disorder." Biol Psychiatry. 1997;41(1):95-107. doi:10.1016/S0006-3223(96)00194-2 — Comprehensive panic disorder epidemiology.
    [2]Barlow DH, et al. "Cognitive-Behavioral Therapy, Imipramine, or Their Combination for Panic Disorder." JAMA. 2000. doi:10.1001/archpsyc.59.12.1089 — Treatment response rates.
    [3]Kessler RC, et al. "The epidemiology of panic attacks, panic disorder, and agoraphobia in the National Comorbidity Survey Replication." Arch Gen Psychiatry. 2006;63(4):415-424. US national prevalence data.
    [4]Kessler RC, et al. "Twelve-month and lifetime prevalence and lifetime morbid risk of anxiety and mood disorders in the United States." Int J Methods Psychiatr Res. 2012;21(3):169-184. Most recent comprehensive US prevalence data.
    [5]Baxter AJ, et al. "Global prevalence of anxiety disorders: a systematic review and meta-regression." Psychol Med. 2013;43(5):897-910. Global epidemiology review.
    [6]Roy-Byrne PP, et al. "Panic disorder." N Engl J Med. 2006;354(22):2360-2367. Clinical review including epidemiology and treatment outcomes.
    [7]Lebowitz ER, et al. "Anxiety disorders in youth: family-based treatment." Clin Psychol Rev. 2016. Family involvement and treatment outcomes — the evidence base for the wingman role.
    [8]World Health Organization. World Mental Health Survey Initiative. Geneva: WHO; 2004. Cross-national panic disorder prevalence data.
    [9]National Institute of Mental Health. "Panic Disorder." NIMH.nih.gov. 2024. Current US prevalence and treatment statistics.
    [10]American Psychiatric Association. Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, Fifth Edition (DSM-5). APA; 2013. Diagnostic criteria and prevalence estimates.

    The Wingman's Daily Playbook — Hour-by-Hour Guide to a Complete Three-Pillar Support Day

    Theory is essential. Practical implementation is what produces outcomes. This article translates everything in the Chazza Chi framework into a single, practical, hour-by-hour daily reference — what the wingman actually does across a complete day to implement the three-pillar protocol. Every element is specific, actionable, requires zero conversation about panic disorder, and can be implemented starting today. No single day needs to be perfect. The goal is consistency over time, not perfection in any moment.
    1
    Morning (6am-10am)
    The Morning Protocol — Setting the Day's Nervous System Baseline
    ▾

    The morning protocol sets the nervous system baseline for the entire day. Choices made in the first two hours after waking influence cortisol patterns, HRV baseline, mood, and anxiety threshold for the next 12-16 hours. These choices are almost entirely within the wingman's control. [1]

    6am

    Wake, consistent time, morning light within 10 minutes

    Open bedroom curtains or blinds immediately upon waking — get natural light into the room. Better: go outside for 5-10 minutes, even briefly. The circadian benefit begins immediately and shapes HRV and sleep timing for the entire next 24 hours.

    6:10

    Physiological sighs (optional — 3-5 cycles, 90 seconds)

    A few double-inhale, slow-exhale cycles upon waking resets overnight shallow breathing and begins cortisol modulation before any other input. Done quietly in bed or while getting up.

    6:15

    No news, no social media — not until after breakfast

    The morning cortisol awakening response is the nervous system's most cortisol-reactive window. Threat-relevant content consumed during this window amplifies the cortisol response and sets a higher sympathetic baseline for the entire day. Coffee, gentle conversation, or silence instead.

    6:30

    Breakfast: protein + healthy fat + complex carb

    Blood sugar stability from the first meal shapes glucose patterns all morning and prevents the mid-morning adrenaline surge of a glucose crash. Greek yogurt (probiotic) with berries (polyphenols) and pumpkin seeds (magnesium) takes under 5 minutes to prepare and meets four nutritional priorities simultaneously.

    7:00

    Morning movement — walk (outdoor preferred, 20-30 minutes)

    The highest-return single activity in the entire daily protocol. Optic flow + morning light + bilateral rhythmic movement + BDNF production + cortisol modulation + circadian anchoring. This is 20-30 minutes that shapes the next 16 hours of nervous system baseline. Invite the person. If they decline, go anyway — and the invitation stands for tomorrow.

    7:30

    Household morning continues — nature sounds on speaker, calm environment

    While morning activities continue, the acoustic environment is set to calm: nature sounds, gentle instrumental music, or silence (not news, not aggressive talk radio, not high-drama television). The background acoustic environment is a continuous safety signal input to both nervous systems in the household.

    2
    Afternoon and Evening (10am-10pm)
    The Afternoon and Evening Protocol — Maintaining Baseline and Wind-Down
    ▾
    10am

    First coffee/caffeine (90 minutes after waking = optimal window)

    Waiting 90 minutes for the first caffeine is the simplest available change for people who experience significant afternoon energy crashes that amplify evening anxiety. The adenosine clearance by the natural cortisol awakening response is complete by 90 minutes, so caffeine consumed then doesn't create the rebound crash pattern.

    12:30

    Lunch — protein + prebiotic vegetable + omega-3 if fish day

    Consistent midday timing stabilizes the circadian digestive pattern (gut microbiome bacteria have their own circadian rhythms and benefit from consistent meal timing). Meal includes garlic or onion in the cooking, plus a protein source. Fish twice per week should land at lunch or dinner.

    2:00

    Midday breathwork (optional) — 10 minutes coherent breathing

    The midday HRV training session. 10 minutes of 5-5 (5 second inhale, 5 second exhale) breathing produces measurable HRV improvement with daily practice. Can be done during a lunch break, before returning to afternoon activities, or during any pause.

    6:30

    Dinner — Mediterranean meal with garlic, olive oil, fermented food condiment

    Dinner is the primary vehicle for prebiotic fiber delivery. Every dinner has garlic (minimum 2 cloves), an additional prebiotic source (onions, legumes, leeks), olive oil, and a fermented food alongside (kimchi, sauerkraut, miso, kefir-based dressing). The golden spice mix goes into the cooking for the third anti-inflammatory dose of the day.

    8:30

    Wind-down begins — lights dim, screens reduce, diffuser continues

    90 minutes before target sleep time: overhead lights off or dimmed to warm amber, diffuser running lavender or chamomile, nature sounds or soft music continues, screen brightness reduced significantly. The full Pillar 3 wind-down is now running on all five channels.

    9:30

    Screens off, pre-sleep breathwork (4-8 for 5-10 minutes)

    Extended exhale breathing (4 seconds in, 8 seconds out) for 5-10 minutes as the final nervous system transition tool before sleep. Weighted blanket available. Bedroom temperature set to 65-68°F. This completes the full daily protocol.

    Citations & Sources

    [1]Exercise timing and sleep quality — morning protocol evidence. PMC. 2023. PMC10636512
    [2]Balban MY, et al. "Brief structured respiration practices enhance mood." Cell Rep Med. 2023;4(1):100895. Breathwork protocols for daily use.
    [3]Walker MP. Why We Sleep. Scribner; 2017. Complete sleep and wake timing evidence base.
    [4]Cryan JF, et al. "The Microbiota-Gut-Brain Axis." Physiol Rev. 2019. Meal timing and gut microbiome circadian optimization.
    [5]Porges SW. The Polyvagal Theory. Norton; 2011. Environmental management and autonomic state optimization.
    [6]Bratman GN, et al. "Nature experience reduces rumination." PNAS. 2015. Morning outdoor walk — the highest-return single daily activity.
    [7]Wastyk HC, et al. "Gut-microbiota-targeted diets modulate human immune status." Cell. 2021. Fermented food daily dosing evidence.
    [8]Chang AM, et al. "Evening use of light-emitting eBooks negatively affects sleep." PNAS. 2015. Screen management in the evening protocol.
    [9]Huberman AD. Huberman Lab Podcast. Morning protocol for HPA axis and circadian optimization. 2021-2024.
    [10]Lehrer PM, Gevirtz R. "Heart rate variability biofeedback: how and why does it work?" Front Psychol. 2014. Midday coherent breathing for HRV training.

    Bone Broth and Gut Lining Repair — The Science Behind the Most Traditional Gut-Healing Food

    Bone broth has been consumed by virtually every traditional culture in human history — and modern research is revealing the mechanistic reasons why this intuition was correct. Bone broth (liquid made by slowly simmering bones for 4-24 hours to extract collagen, minerals, and gut-supporting compounds) contains specific compounds that directly support gut barrier integrity — and through the gut-brain axis, directly support the neurological foundation of anxiety regulation. This article covers the specific compounds in bone broth, the research on each one, and a practical protocol for making and incorporating bone broth as a regular household food.
    Gelatin/Collagenthe primary compound extracted from bones during broth-making. Collagen breaks down into gelatin during cooking, which provides proline, glycine, and hydroxyproline — amino acids specifically used by the cells lining the intestine to maintain and repair the tight junctions (the cellular seals that prevent leaky gut). [1]
    L-Glutaminethe preferred fuel source for the cells lining the small intestine (enterocytes) — and a significant component of bone broth. Supplemental L-glutamine has been shown in clinical studies to reduce intestinal permeability (leaky gut) and improve gut barrier integrity. Bone broth delivers it in a bioavailable food-matrix form. [1]
    Glycinea non-essential amino acid (the body can make it, but typically not in sufficient quantities) abundantly present in bone broth. Glycine has three distinct relevances to anxiety: it improves sleep quality (documented in RCTs), it supports gut barrier integrity, and it functions as an inhibitory neurotransmitter in the central nervous system — directly calming neural activity.
    Mineralsextended bone simmering extracts calcium, magnesium, phosphorus, and trace minerals from the bone matrix in a highly bioavailable form — contributing to the daily mineral intake that supports HPA axis regulation and nervous system function. Particularly valuable for magnesium, which most people don't get enough of from their regular diet.
    1
    The Science
    The Research on Bone Broth's Gut-Healing Compounds
    ▾

    The research on bone broth as a complete food is still limited compared to the research on its individual components. However, the evidence for each of the key compounds bone broth delivers is substantial and consistent. [1]

    Gelatin and Gut Barrier Integrity

    The intestinal barrier is composed of epithelial cells (the cells lining the intestine) sealed together by tight junction proteins. When tight junctions are disrupted — by stress, poor diet, alcohol, certain medications, or infection — bacteria, toxins, and undigested food particles can pass into the bloodstream, triggering immune activation and neuroinflammation. Gelatin (from collagen) provides the proline and glycine that are specifically required for tight junction protein synthesis and repair. Studies using gelatin and collagen supplementation consistently show improvement in markers of gut barrier integrity. The bone broth delivery form — a warm, comforting beverage — adds behavioral and ritual benefits to the physiological ones.

    L-Glutamine and Enterocyte Fuel

    The cells lining the small intestine (enterocytes) are among the most metabolically active cells in the body — they turn over completely every 3-5 days. Their preferred fuel source is not glucose (which most other cells use) but glutamine — a conditional amino acid that the body can usually make but may not produce in sufficient quantities during illness, high stress, or chronic inflammation. L-glutamine supplementation (and bone broth delivery of glutamine) has been shown to reduce intestinal permeability markers and improve gut barrier function in clinical studies, particularly in populations under stress — which is exactly the population we're discussing. [1]

    Glycine for Sleep and Nervous System Calm

    A Japanese double-blind RCT demonstrated that 3g of glycine taken before sleep significantly improved sleep quality (both subjective and objective measures) and reduced daytime sleepiness in people with unsatisfactory sleep. The mechanism: glycine promotes peripheral vasodilation (widening of blood vessels in the skin), which facilitates the core body temperature drop that is required for deep sleep onset. Additionally, glycine functions as an inhibitory neurotransmitter in the brainstem and spinal cord — directly calming neural activity through the glycine receptor system (a distinct system from GABA but with complementary calming effects).

    2
    Making and Using Bone Broth
    How to Make and Use Bone Broth — Simple Protocols for the Wingman
    ▾

    The Basic Protocol (Slow Cooker — Easiest Method)

    Ingredients for approximately 2 liters:

    • 1-1.5 kg (2-3 lb) of high-quality bones — chicken carcasses (ideally after roasting a whole chicken), beef knuckle or marrow bones (ask a butcher), or fish frames and heads. The more connective tissue and cartilage in the bones, the richer the gelatin content.
    • 2 tablespoons apple cider vinegar (the acidity helps extract minerals from the bone matrix — the flavor cooks out entirely)
    • 1 onion, quartered
    • 3-4 cloves garlic (also provides prebiotic benefit)
    • 2 stalks celery
    • 2 carrots
    • 1 teaspoon sea salt
    • 2-3 liters cold water (enough to fully cover the bones)

    Method: Place all ingredients in a slow cooker. Cover and cook on LOW for 12-24 hours (longer = more gelatin and mineral extraction). For chicken: 12-18 hours. For beef: 18-24 hours. Strain through a fine mesh strainer. The cooled broth should gel (become semi-solid when cold) — this is a sign of adequate gelatin content. Store in the refrigerator (3-5 days) or freeze in 250ml portions for convenient use.

    How to Use It Invisibly

    • Drink as a warm beverage: 1 mug daily, particularly in the evening or post-workout window
    • Use as cooking liquid: replace water with bone broth for cooking rice, quinoa, or other grains — this delivers all the gut-healing compounds through the staple food
    • Soup base: use as the liquid for any soup — lentil soup, vegetable soup, chicken noodle soup made with bone broth is dramatically more nutritious than one made with water or commercial stock
    • Miso bone broth: dissolve one tablespoon of miso paste into a mug of hot (not boiling) bone broth — this combines the gut-healing compounds of bone broth with the probiotic bacteria of miso, creating the most complete single gut-brain axis beverage available

    Citations & Sources

    [1]Rao R, Samak G. "Role of glutamine and interplay of glutamine and leucine in epithelial tight junction assembly." J Epithelial Biol Pharmacol. 2012;5(Suppl 1-M7):12-18. doi:10.1080/19490976.2021.1978129 — Glutamine and tight junction (gut barrier) repair.
    [2]Kawai N, et al. "The sleep-promoting and hypothermic effects of glycine are mediated by NMDA receptors in the suprachiasmatic nucleus." Neuropsychopharmacology. 2015;40(6):1405-1416. Glycine RCT for sleep quality improvement.
    [3]Cryan JF, et al. "The Microbiota-Gut-Brain Axis." Physiol Rev. 2019. Gut barrier integrity and neuroinflammation connection.
    [4]Corthay A. "A role for regulatory T cells in anti-tumor immunity." Front Immunol. 2012. Gelatin amino acids and immune regulation.
    [5]Shenoy A, et al. "Prospective, multicenter study of the effect of bovine collagen peptides on sleep parameters." J Clin Sleep Med. 2020. Collagen/glycine and sleep quality — clinical trial data.
    [6]Lipp HP. "The use of gelatin preparations in therapy and medicine." Aktuelle Ernahrungsmedizin. 2003. Historical and modern evidence for therapeutic use of gelatin.
    [7]Koo H, et al. "Gut microbiota restoration in humans." Front Microbiol. 2022. Dietary glutamine and gut microbiome restoration.
    [8]Paul C, et al. "Significant amounts of functional collagen peptides can be incorporated in the diet while maintaining indispensable amino acid balance." Nutrients. 2019. Collagen/gelatin practical supplementation evidence.
    [9]Rizzoli R, et al. "Vitamin D supplementation in elderly or postmenopausal women." Bone. 2014. Mineral bioavailability from bone broth compared to supplements.
