    Polyvagal Theory, developed by Stephen Porges, identifies the ventral vagal complex as the neural pathway that supports social engagement and co-regulation. When the ventral vagal system is active, the person emits safety cues — soft facial expressions, prosodic (melodic, non-threatening) voice tone, relaxed posture — that directly activate the ventral vagal system in nearby people through the process called neuroception (the nervous system's subconscious detection of safety or danger from social cues). [39]

    Research shows that co-regulation through attuned presence reduces cortisol by approximately 30%, increases vagal tone, and builds secure attachment patterns. [40] When individuals feel safe and connected in another person's presence, the vagus nerve is activated, promoting relaxation and recovery. [41] The nervous system's autonomic state is significantly influenced by proximity to regulated others — a dynamic that persists throughout the lifespan. [42]

    This is why the wingman's own three pillars matter so completely: The wingman who is well-rested, well-nourished, physically active, and living in a calm sensory environment maintains high vagal tone, low cortisol, and a genuine ventral vagal state. Their nervous system continuously emits safety cues that the person with panic disorder's threat-monitoring system receives and responds to. A burned-out, depleted, dysregulated wingman cannot emit these cues — and the person's nervous system detects the absence of safety signals. You cannot fake co-regulation. You have to actually be regulated.

    What Co-Regulation Looks Like in Practice

    • Your voice: Slow, warm, prosodic (varied tone) voice activates the social engagement system. Flat, urgent, tense, or rapid speech activates the threat system. During panic or distress, speak as if reading a bedtime story — slowly, warmly, with gentle variation.
    • Your face: A relaxed, warm facial expression sends safety cues through the ventral vagal pathway. Worried, furrowed, anxious expressions send threat cues. The person's nervous system is reading your face continuously, below conscious awareness.
    • Your breathing: Your own breathing pattern influences the person's through entrainment. Breathe slowly and deeply — visibly, audibly. This is not performance; it is direct nervous system communication.
    • Your posture: Open, relaxed, oriented toward the person. Not hovering, not backing away, not tense. The body's posture broadcasts autonomic state continuously through proprioceptive and visual pathways.
    • Your predictability: The threat system registers novel, unpredictable input as potential danger. A wingman who responds to panic consistently, predictably, and the same way each time provides a secondary safety signal: "this environment is reliable and safe."

    The Absorption Risk — When You Catch the Anxiety

    Emotional contagion — catching another person's emotional state — is the downside of the same co-regulation mechanism that makes calm presence beneficial. A wingman who enters every interaction with the person already vigilant, scanning for signs of impending panic, anxious about being enough help, or dreading the next crisis — is not providing co-regulation. They are providing co-dysregulation. Their anxious nervous system is adding to the anxious signal the person is already receiving from their own threat system. [43]

    This is the deepest reason why the research on caregiver wellbeing matters so clinically: a burned-out, overwhelmed wingman becomes an anxiety amplifier rather than a regulator. Recognizing the signs of your own emotional contagion — increased vigilance about the person's state, hypervigilance about potential triggers, anxiety about their anxiety — is the first step in addressing it. The wingman who notices they have caught the anxiety needs to process it through their own support system, not suppress it and continue.

    7
    Acute Response
    During a Panic Attack — The Evidence-Based Response Protocol
    ▾

    A panic attack is a medical event that looks like an emergency and is not one. The prefrontal cortex — the rational, language-processing, instruction-receiving brain region — has reduced functionality when the amygdala's alarm is at full activation. This is documented neurobiology, not a figure of speech. Instructions, explanations, and logical arguments will not be processed effectively during full panic escalation. What will be processed: tone, presence, physical contact, and modeled physiological state. [44]

    The fundamental shift: The wingman who understands this stops trying to talk the person out of panic and starts being the nervous system cue the person's brain can actually receive. You are not solving a cognitive problem. You are providing a social safety signal to a threat-detection system that is temporarily offline from rational input.

    The Acute Response Protocol — Step by Step

    1

    Stay physically calm — your state is contagious

    Check your own state first. If you're anxious about the panic, regulate yourself before engaging. A visible, deep slow breath of your own takes 5 seconds and resets your own ventral vagal tone — and models the breathing you want the person to see. A calm, regulated wingman is the most powerful intervention available at this moment. [45]

    2

    Get close — but check before touching

    "Can I hold your hand?" or "Do you want me to sit next to you?" gives the person control over physical contact during a moment when everything feels out of control. Some people need touch. Some find it overwhelming. The pre-agreed plan (established during a calm moment) should already tell you which. Absent that, ask. [46]

    3

    Model breathing visibly — don't instruct it

    Breathing instructions require prefrontal cortex processing: parsing language, formulating intent, executing a new motor pattern. None of this is available under full panic activation. Instead: breathe the physiological sigh yourself — double inhale through the nose, long exhale through the mouth — slowly and visibly. The mirror neuron system will often synchronize naturally, without instruction. [47]

    4

    Short, warm, predictable phrases only

    "I'm here." "You're safe." "This will pass." Repeated, slowly, in the same calm tone. Short. Simple. Predictable. Do not check in anxiously ("Are you okay? Are you okay?") — repeated checking signals concern and confirms the emergency. Say the same simple phrases in the same calm tone and repeat them. The pattern of predictability is itself a safety cue. [48]

    5

    Let the panic run its natural course

    Panic attacks have a physiological ceiling — the sympathetic system cannot sustain maximum activation indefinitely. Adrenaline metabolizes within 20–30 minutes. The person will come through the other side. The wingman's job is to stay calm and present while this happens, confirming through their steady presence that the person is safe and the situation is survivable. Rushing the person, urgently trying to stop the panic, or calling emergency services (unless genuinely warranted) creates secondary alarm. [49]

    6

    Have a pre-agreed plan

    Ask, in a calm moment: "What helps most when you're in the middle of one? What do you need from me?" Some people want touch, some want silence, some want to leave the location, some need to stay and complete the task, some want specific phrases. Documenting the pre-agreed plan removes decision-making burden from both people during the acute episode. [50]

    What to Never Say During a Panic Attack

    • "Calm down." — Communicates the person's state is unacceptable and that calming is a voluntary choice they're failing to make. Amplifies shame. [49]
    • "Just breathe." — Useless instruction without modeling. The instruction requires processing capacity that isn't available. If breathing is the intervention, breathe yourself.
    • "There's nothing to be scared of." — Invalidation. There is something happening — the threat system is firing at maximum. The problem is not that the threat is real; the problem is the misfiring. These are different things that require different responses.
    • "You're embarrassing yourself / us." — Creates shame about the panic attack, which activates a secondary panic about the panic. The worst possible intervention. [51]
    • "Why does this keep happening?" — Demands explanation during a moment when explanation is neurologically impossible. This question is appropriate in a calm moment. During the attack, it is a demand the person cannot meet and will attempt to meet through more anxiety.

    What Happens After the Attack Passes

    The period immediately after a panic attack is not the time for processing, analysis, or problem-solving. The person is physiologically exhausted — adrenaline has metabolized, the sympathetic system has downregulated, and the body needs recovery time. The post-attack conversation that immediately jumps to "we need to talk about what just happened" recreates the cortisol and social pressure that the nervous system is trying to recover from.

    The appropriate post-attack response: quiet, calm, comfortable presence. Water. Physical comfort. No analysis. If they want to talk, listen without solving. If they want silence, provide it. The problem-solving conversation about "what can we do differently" — including talking about treatment, the three pillars, or making any plans — belongs in a calm moment that is not the same hour as the panic attack.

    One specific exception: if the panic attack occurred in a public place or a situation the person successfully completed despite the panic — quietly noting this later ("You stayed through that. That was impressive.") provides non-theatrical positive reinforcement that the nervous system actually benefited from the exposure. Not immediately after. Not during. Later, briefly, without making it a bigger deal than it was.

    8
    Three Pillars
    The Wingman's Three-Pillar Application — The Daily Practice of Invisible Support
    ▾

    The three pillars of Chazza Chi — nutrition, movement, and environment — are not interventions you administer to someone. They are conditions you create around someone. The wingman who understands this stops asking "how do I get them to eat better / exercise more / use lavender" and starts asking "how do I make the better option the default option?" Environment design answers that question, and the gut-brain axis and nervous system respond to what's provided whether or not the person is participating consciously.

    Pillar 1 — The Kitchen Is Your Territory

    • Change the oils first, without announcement. Swap to extra virgin olive oil and avocado oil across all cooking. Every meal cooked at home becomes anti-inflammatory without a single conversation about gut health or anxiety.
    • Build the spice base in. Garlic, ginger, turmeric, and black pepper go into the pan while the oil heats, before anything else. This is how you cook now. No explanation. The anti-inflammatory and BDNF-supporting effects accumulate daily.
    • Introduce fermented foods invisibly. Kefir blended into smoothies. Sauerkraut as a side condiment. Miso stirred into sauces. The microbiome changes. The gut-brain axis benefits. The person never needs to have a conversation about probiotics.
    • Control what's available. Washed berries in a visible bowl. Mixed nuts in the snack spot. The processed options gone. The decision architecture changes. What's there gets eaten.

    Pillar 2 — Conditions Creator, Not Personal Trainer

    • "I'm going for a walk — want to come?" Not a prescription. Not "you should exercise." State what you're doing and leave the invitation open, without disappointment when declined, without celebration when accepted. The invitation normalizes the activity.
    • Notice the flow states and protect them. Fishing, gardening, woodworking, cooking, driving — activities that produce genuine absorption. Guard time for them. Support the conditions. Never interrupt them.
    • Move alongside, not at. Walking together on the same path. Stretching in the same room. The side-by-side is less performance-pressured than being observed.
    • Bring equipment into the environment. A yoga mat left out. Resistance bands in the living room. A treadmill you use. The barrier to entry drops when the tools are already there and normalized by your use of them.

    Pillar 3 — The Thermostat Approach

    • Run the diffuser. Lavender, bergamot, cedarwood. No explanation needed. It just smells nice. The amygdala responds whether or not the person knows why the house smells different.
    • Change the lighting progressively. Warm-toned LEDs. Lamps instead of overhead. Dimmers in the evening. These happen over days — by the time the nervous system is responding to the change, the person has stopped noticing the change.
    • Manage the acoustic environment. The TV lower. Background music at 60 BPM or below. Device notifications silenced. News off during shared meals. None of this requires a conversation.
    • Bedroom as recovery infrastructure. 65–68°F. Weighted blanket accessible. Lavender linen spray on pillows. Warm lighting. Every night, this environment does nervous system recovery work silently.
    • Open windows. "It's a nice day" covers the CO₂ management. The nervous system doesn't care about the explanation.

    When the Triangle Is Fully Active — The Most Powerful Moments in the Wingman's Day

    A morning outdoor walk: Pillar 2 (aerobic movement, optic flow, BDNF, vagal tone, interoceptive exposure) + Pillar 3 (circadian anchoring light, nature sounds, phytoncides, fresh air) + relationship (co-regulation through shared calm presence). This 30-minute activity is the single highest-leverage Chazza Chi convergence available without equipment, expense, or explanation.

    A meal with medicinal spices, eaten slowly in a calm room: Pillar 1 (gut-brain axis support, anti-inflammatory profile) + Pillar 3 (warm light, soft sound, comfortable seating). Parasympathetic digestion — which only occurs in a rest-and-digest state — absorbs nutrients more effectively than sympathetic digestion. Pillars 1 and 3 compound each other in every shared meal that is eaten calmly.

    Evening gentle movement with calming scent and music: Pillar 2 (proprioceptive vagal activation, breathwork opportunity, sleep quality improvement) + Pillar 3 (dim warm lighting, lavender, slow-tempo music). The movement deepens the parasympathetic response; the environmental design deepens the movement's calming effect. Neither is as effective alone.

    The bedroom at night: Pillar 2 (sleep recovery, BDNF integration, HRV restoration) + Pillar 3 (temperature, scent, darkness, weighted blanket). The environment determines how much recovery happens. A poorly designed sleep environment undermines everything the day's movement and nutrition built.

    9
    Caregiver Burden
    Caregiver Burden, Compassion Fatigue & the Risk Nobody Warns Wingmen About
    ▾

    Here is the statistic that is consistently left out of support resources for families of people with anxiety disorders: 26% of caregivers develop clinically significant anxiety symptoms themselves. Those supporting individuals with significant mental health and behavioral needs face even higher risk. Depression and anxiety are reported in 52–94% of family caregivers in some studies. [52]

    Anxiety and depression in caregivers predict higher stress; higher stress predicts higher caregiver burden; higher caregiver burden is associated with significantly higher anxiety — a self-reinforcing spiral that the research reliably documents and that untreated produces acute caregiver breakdown. [53] Caregivers with higher burden also show higher rates of cognitive fusion and experiential avoidance — the same psychological patterns found in the person with anxiety disorder. [54]

    Compassion fatigue is the convergence of secondary traumatic stress and cumulative burnout produced by sustained exposure to another person's distress without adequate personal replenishment. Professionals regularly exposed to traumatic experiences — including healthcare workers, emergency responders, and mental health providers — are well-studied for this. The patterns in informal caregivers are similar: gradual emotional numbing, reduced capacity for empathy, increased irritability, physical exhaustion, and the dissolution of the calm, regulated presence that makes caregiving effective. [55]

    Warning Signs in the Wingman

    • You've started scanning for signs of impending panic rather than living normally in your own life — your daily state has become primarily about managing another person's state
    • You've contracted your own life to manage theirs — fewer social activities, less time with your own friends, activities you've given up to be available
    • You feel resentment, then guilt about the resentment — the classic caregiver burnout cycle [56]
    • Your own sleep, nutrition, and movement have significantly deteriorated — you're not applying the three pillars to yourself
    • You believe you are the only thing standing between the person and disaster — a level of responsibility that is unsustainable and untrue
    • You feel selfish when you take time for yourself — a significant predictor of secondary traumatic stress [57]

    Mindfulness, ACT, and Evidence-Based Support for Wingmen

    Mindfulness-Based Stress Reduction (MBSR): An RCT of MBSR for family caregivers of people with psychiatric conditions found statistically significant decreases in state anxiety, trait anxiety, hopelessness, and caregiver burden, alongside significant increases in self-compassion. Gains maintained at 1-month and 3-month follow-up. [58] A web-based mindfulness intervention for families living with mental illness found significant improvements in mindfulness and self-compassion, and significant decreases in perceived stress and caregiver burden dimensions. [59]

    Acceptance and Commitment Therapy (ACT) for Caregivers: A meta-analysis of 24 studies found moderate effects of ACT on depressive symptoms and quality of life, and small to moderate effects on stress in family caregivers. [60] A comprehensive review of 29 RCTs confirmed sustained improvements in psychological flexibility, anxiety, depression, and stress symptoms persisting up to 6 months post-intervention. [61] ACT for parents of anxious youth has shown success in improving psychological flexibility and well-being. [62]

    The practical takeaway: MBSR apps (Headspace, Calm, Insight Timer), ACT-based self-help books (Russ Harris' "The Happiness Trap"), and therapist-led individual or group programs all offer evidence-based tools for the wingman's own mental health maintenance. These are not luxuries — they are the foundation of sustained effective support.

    10
    Communication
    The Wingman's Communication Guide — What Works, What Doesn't, and Why
    ▾

    The research on communication in relationships affected by anxiety disorders is consistent: how the wingman communicates during distress is more important than what they say. Tone, pace, predictability, and the emotional quality of the communication all reach the nervous system before the content of the words does. The principles below are derived from the clinical literature on panic disorder, expressed emotion, co-regulation, and family systems research.

    Six Evidence-Based Communication Principles

    1

    Short sentences — brevity is kindness

    Language-processing capacity decreases when anxiety is elevated. Short sentences are received; long explanations are not. "I'm here" carries more meaning in high-anxiety moments than "I want you to know I understand what you're going through and am committed to your recovery." Speak in units of 5–10 words when the person is activated. [48]

    2

    Slow, warm, even tone — the voice is the message

    The nervous system processes tone before content. A slow, warm, prosodic voice activates the ventral vagal social engagement system. An anxious, urgent, or frustrated tone activates the threat system. Speak as you'd speak to someone you don't want to startle. The content of what you say matters far less than how you say it. [39]

    3

    Validate before suggesting — always, without exception

    "That sounds really hard" must come before "have you tried..." Every time. Without exception. The person's experience must be acknowledged before any suggestion, solution, or information will be received as caring rather than dismissive. Suggestions before validation read as "stop feeling that way and do this instead."

    4

    Ask rather than assume — the person knows their experience

    "What would help right now?" is more useful than assuming you know the answer. The research on panic attack support consistently identifies asking about needs as a core best practice — the person's experience of what helps is more accurate than the wingman's inference. [51]

    5

    No problem-solving during distress — timing is everything

    Analysis, planning, treatment conversations, and "you should try..." belong in calm moments. During or immediately after a distressing episode, they add cognitive and social load to a recovering nervous system. "Can we talk about this when things are calmer?" is not avoidance — it is appropriate timing.

    6

    Never express frustration in the moment

    Frustration activates shame. Shame activates sympathetic dominance. Sympathetic dominance worsens panic disorder. If frustration is building — and it will, this is a long arc — it belongs in your own support system, your own therapy, and your own processing. Not in the moment with the person. [63]

    Building the Shared Language in Advance

    One of the most practical and underused communication tools available is the panic response plan: a conversation held in a calm moment that establishes what the person specifically needs during a panic episode. Research on couples managing anxiety consistently supports developing shared communication systems around anxiety episodes before they occur. [50]

    Questions to establish and document: Do you want me to touch you, and if so how? Do you want to be spoken to or does silence help more? Is there a phrase you find regulating to hear? What's our signal that you need space versus need me closer? If we're in public, what's the plan? What should I definitely not do? Are you okay with me modeling the breathing technique, or does that feel condescending?

    These answers vary dramatically between individuals. What one person needs (hand-holding, repetition of "I'm here") is actively dysregulating for another (who needs silence and physical space). The pre-agreed plan removes the guesswork and gives both people structure during structureless moments. Review it periodically as what helps may shift as the disorder evolves.

    11
    The Long Game
    Setting Limits, Tracking Progress & When to Involve Professionals
    ▾

    Panic disorder recovery is measured in months and years. The wingman who understands this doesn't interpret a bad week as failure, doesn't celebrate a good month as cure, and maintains a steady long-view approach through the inevitable cycles. That consistency — the long, unbroken arc of support that doesn't escalate with worsening or over-celebrate with improvement — is itself one of the most therapeutic things the wingman provides.

    Setting Appropriate Limits on Accommodation

    The wingman cannot sustainably absorb unlimited accommodation demands, and the research shows that unlimited accommodation worsens the disorder. At some point, limits are necessary — not as punishment, but as the boundary of a sustainable relationship and the signal of confidence in the person's capacity. The SPACE framework articulates the communication: "I love you. I'm not going to [accommodation] anymore. Not because I don't care — because I do. I believe you can handle this, and I'm going to be right here with you while you do." [13]

    What Genuine Progress Looks Like

    Recovery signs are quiet and gradual. The wingman who knows what to look for won't miss them:

    • The person mentions physical sensations from exertion with neutrality rather than alarm — mild curiosity instead of fear
    • They initiate previously avoided activities without being asked or invited
    • Recovery from difficult moments becomes faster — the system still activates, but returns to baseline more quickly
    • The person begins using the past tense: "I used to not be able to..." rather than "I can't..."
    • Life's territory expands — places visited, activities attempted, social engagements completed that were previously impossible
    • They talk about the panic disorder as something they're managing, not something that's managing them

    None of these warrant theatrical celebration from the wingman. Quietly noting them — "I noticed you drove there yourself" — provides non-pressured positive reinforcement that confirms the progress without making it a performance. The goal is building an internal sense of capacity, not an external audience.

    When Professional Help Is the Right Next Step

    • Panic disorder present for years without meaningful improvement — CBT, particularly Panic Control Treatment, has substantial evidence base [64]
    • Significant comorbid depression — requires clinical evaluation; changes the treatment approach and risk profile [9]
    • Severe agoraphobia that has significantly contracted the person's world — requires formal exposure hierarchy and clinical guidance beyond natural life activities
    • Relationship significantly damaged by the panic disorder — couples therapy alongside individual treatment addresses both simultaneously [65]
    • Suicidal ideation — immediate professional involvement; call 988 (Suicide and Crisis Lifeline)

    The Non-Confrontational Introduction to Professional Support

    "I've been reading about this thing called Panic Control Treatment — it's a specific CBT approach designed for exactly what you experience. I was wondering if it might be worth trying alongside what we're already doing." — This frames it as additional resource, not admission of failure. It names the specific evidence-based treatment rather than the generic "you should see a therapist."

    "I've been thinking about seeing someone myself. Would you want to come with me to a session sometime, or would you rather I ask about referrals for someone you could see separately?" — Normalizes professional support by making it bilateral rather than positioning the person as the patient.

    The research on treatment initiation consistently shows that warm, specific, non-pressured suggestions from trusted people in the person's life are among the most effective pathways to treatment engagement. The wingman who frames clinical treatment as "something that could help you move faster toward the life you want" rather than "something that will fix your problem" is more likely to support voluntary treatment engagement.

    12
    The Wingman's Own
    The Wingman's Own Three Pillars — Your Stability Is the Foundation of Theirs
    ▾

    The research literature on caregiver wellbeing is explicit on this point: the wingman's own mental and physical health is a direct determinant of the quality of support they provide. This is not a self-care platitude. It is a documented causal relationship. A wingman who is depleted, burned out, physically unwell, and poorly regulated cannot maintain the calm, consistent, non-reactive, low-expressed-emotion presence that the research predicts produces the best outcomes. Their dysregulation actively interferes with the person's recovery through the co-regulation pathways that work in both directions. [66]

    The three pillars apply to the wingman as much as to the person with panic disorder. Your gut health determines your neurotransmitter baseline and your capacity for emotional regulation. Your movement determines your vagal tone, your cortisol clearance, and your resilience to the chronic stress of the caregiving role. Your sensory environment determines your sleep quality, your HRV, and your ability to enter conversations in a regulated state. Your three pillars are not separate from the work — they are the work, applied to yourself.

    The Wingman's Support Resources

    • NAMI Family-to-Family — free, 8-session evidence-based program. Online and in-person. Covers anxiety disorders explicitly. [67]
    • Al-Anon Family Groups — 12-step program whose principles translate directly to any family affected by a chronic condition that reorganizes the family around the person's illness. Available virtually everywhere. [4]
    • Individual therapy — the wingman working with their own therapist is entirely consistent with all evidence-based family support models and is not in any way a sign of failure
    • Couple's therapy — when panic disorder has significantly damaged the relationship, couple's therapy alongside individual treatment addresses both the anxiety and the relational impact simultaneously; partner-assisted CBT for agoraphobia has specific research support across multiple studies [3]
    • Online caregiver communities — peer support through shared experience provides the Al-Anon normalization effect without requiring local attendance
    • Mindfulness and ACT resources — MBSR significantly reduces caregiver anxiety, hopelessness, and burden in RCTs; ACT produces moderate-to-large effects on depression and quality of life in caregivers [61]

    The final thing to say, because it is the thing that the research across every tradition in this article arrives at independently: the wingman who takes care of themselves is not being selfish. They are being effective. The person you are supporting needs a regulated, healthy, present wingman more than they need a depleted, resentful, burned-out one. Caring for yourself is caring for them. The triangle holds when all three sides are maintained — and that includes the wingman's own side of the triangle.

    ★
    FAQ
    Frequently Asked Questions — What Wingmen Ask Most Often
    ▾

    Q: How do I know if what I'm doing is helping or making things worse?

    The clearest indicators that you are helping rather than hurting: (1) the person is engaging in more activities over time rather than fewer, (2) their panic attack frequency is stable or declining, (3) they are seeking less reassurance from you rather than more, (4) their world is expanding rather than contracting. If you observe the opposite — increasing avoidance, increasing dependence on you for managing situations, more frequent panic — the approach needs to change. Return to the sections on accommodation (family behavioral adjustments that maintain avoidance) and expressed emotion (family communication patterns) in this article and assess honestly whether you have drifted toward enabling avoidance rather than supporting engagement. [14]

    Q: The person refuses therapy. Is the Chazza Chi framework still useful?

    Yes — and in some ways the three-pillar framework is more immediately actionable without clinical involvement than CBT (Cognitive Behavioral Therapy) is. Dietary changes, movement introduction, and environmental design all work whether or not the person is in therapy. The wingman can implement all three pillars without the person's participation or even awareness. What the framework cannot replace is the interoceptive exposure (gradual, safe experience of physical sensations) and cognitive restructuring (changing unhelpful thinking patterns) that formal CBT provides. If the person won't pursue therapy, the three pillars become the primary intervention — while maintaining a relationship that preserves the possibility of therapy in the future. [19]

    Q: What do I do during an active panic attack?

    During an active panic attack, less is more. The most effective wingman response: physical calm presence (sitting close, a hand on the back or arm if welcome), minimal verbal communication, and modeling the physiological sigh breath (double inhale through nose, long slow exhale) without directing the person to breathe. Do not say "calm down" — it never works and usually escalates. Do not ask "what's wrong?" — questions require cognitive processing the person cannot do well during panic. Do not provide extensive reassurance about physical safety — reassurance maintains the cycle. Say, quietly and once: "I'm right here. This will pass." Then stay present and physically calm, breathing slowly yourself, until the peak passes. Your nervous system's calm directly influences theirs through co-regulation (the biological process of one calm nervous system calming another). [17]

    Q: Is it normal to feel resentful or burned out? What do I do about it?

    Yes — entirely normal, and important to address honestly rather than push through. Caregiver burnout (exhaustion from supporting someone without adequate support for yourself) in panic disorder wingmen is common, documented, and clinically significant. Burned-out wingmen become inconsistent, emotionally reactive, and swing between over-accommodation and frustrated withdrawal — both of which make panic disorder worse. The Al-Anon model (the peer support framework that inspired Anxiety Wingman) built its entire program around this reality: the people doing the supporting need their own support. Your wellbeing is not a luxury — it is a prerequisite for effective, sustained wingman presence. [4]

    Q: The person is on medication. Will the three pillars still help?

    Yes — and the evidence suggests the three pillars and pharmaceutical treatment are complementary rather than competing. SSRIs (the most commonly prescribed medications for panic disorder) work on serotonin reuptake (keeping more serotonin in the brain). The dietary pillar works on serotonin production in the gut (providing more serotonin to work with). The movement pillar works on BDNF (which enhances serotonin pathway effectiveness). The environmental pillar reduces the chronic cortisol (stress hormone) load that works against everything else. None of these mechanisms counteract SSRI function — they complement it. The three pillars are not alternatives to medication or therapy. They are additional sides of a stronger overall structure. [53]

    Q: How long until the three-pillar approach produces noticeable results?

    Dietary changes take 4-12 weeks to produce meaningful shifts in gut microbiome (gut bacteria community) composition and neurotransmitter production. Regular exercise produces neurological improvements (BDNF, HRV, cortisol) measurable within 4-8 weeks. Environmental changes that affect sleep quality produce improvements within days to weeks of consistent implementation. The compounding effect of all three pillars working simultaneously shortens timelines. Most wingmen who implement all three consistently report noticing a shift in the person's baseline within 6-12 weeks — not dramatic reversal, but a measurable increase in good days and a decrease in bad day intensity. [19]

    14
    In the Moment
    The Panic Attack Response Protocol — What to Do Second by Second
    ▾

    This is the most time-sensitive skill in the entire framework. When a panic attack begins — whether you see it coming or it arrives suddenly — everything you do in the next few minutes either helps the nervous system recover faster or makes the episode worse and longer. Most wingmen who have not been taught what to do will follow their instincts. Almost everything instinctive makes panic attacks worse. [14]

    The three most important things to never say during a panic attack:

    • "Calm down." — Impossible to follow. Never works. Adds the stressor of failing to comply. May increase shame and frustration which raises the panic higher.
    • "There's nothing to worry about." — The person knows logically there's nothing to worry about. That knowledge is not accessible to the amygdala firing the emergency response. This response makes the person feel unheard and misunderstood, which adds emotional distress on top of physiological distress.
    • "Is this another panic attack?" — Questions require cognitive processing. During a panic attack, the prefrontal cortex (thinking brain) is partially offline — the amygdala has taken over. Asking diagnostic questions adds cognitive load at the worst possible moment and can be interpreted as impatience or skepticism. [101]

    The Step-by-Step Panic Response Protocol

    1

    Move closer — but don't hover (Seconds 0-30)

    Position yourself near the person — close enough that they know you're there, not so close that it feels smothering. If you're in a crowded space, gently guide them toward a wall, a corner, or a quieter area. Reducing the visual stimulation field (less happening in their visual peripheral vision) directly reduces the sensory load on the already-overwhelmed nervous system. Sit if they're sitting. Stand near them if they're standing. Match their physical level. [17]

    2

    One sentence. Then silence (Seconds 30-60)

    Say once, quietly and without urgency: "I'm right here. This will pass." Then stop talking. This single sentence does three things: it confirms your presence (activating co-regulation — the process of your calm nervous system helping calm theirs), it acknowledges the experience without catastrophizing, and it predicts a safe outcome. Do not repeat it. Additional words add cognitive demand. [16]

    3

    Model the breath — don't instruct it (Minutes 1-5)

    Begin doing the physiological sigh (double nose inhale, long slow exhale — the body's fastest natural calming breath) yourself. Breathe visibly and audibly, but not dramatically. Slow, deep, long exhale. Don't say "breathe with me" — this turns a calming influence into an instruction the person must comply with, adding pressure. Simply model the breath clearly enough that the person can naturally synchronize with it if their nervous system is ready to. This uses the biological principle of respiratory co-regulation (synchronized breathing between nearby people — a natural nervous system calming mechanism). [102]

    4

    Regulate yourself (Entire episode)

    Your nervous system's state directly affects the person's through co-regulation pathways. If you are anxious, alarmed, frustrated, or helpless, your body language, voice tone, and physiological state transmit that — and the nervous system in panic reads all of it. Regulate yourself first. Take your own physiological sigh. Consciously relax your shoulders and jaw. Slow your own heart rate intentionally through slow exhales. A calm wingman is not merely emotionally supportive — they are a biological nervous system resource. [103]

    5

    After the peak passes — minimal processing (Minutes 5-30)

    Panic attacks peak and subside — always. After the acute phase passes, the person will often feel exhausted, embarrassed, or fragile. The instinct is to discuss what happened, reassure extensively, or problem-solve. Resist all of this immediately. The most helpful post-panic response: simple normalizing and practical forward motion. "Want some water?" or "Let's sit for a few minutes" is ideal. Extensive post-panic conversation about the panic attack itself — analyzing it, reassuring about it, discussing what triggered it — maintains focus on the panic and delays return to normal nervous system tone. Less processing immediately after is better. Return to normal activity as smoothly as possible. [104]

    Handling Panic in Situations with No Wingman Script

    Public panic (store, restaurant, event): The priority is environmental simplification and exit if needed — without making the exit itself dramatic. "Hey, let's step outside for a minute" is better than "Are you okay? Should we leave?" Moving toward a wall or a quieter part of the space reduces the sensory field. If the person needs to leave entirely, leave — and frame it as your choice, not theirs ("I'm actually kind of ready to go, want to head out?"). This preserves the person's dignity and reduces the shame of having the panic "win." However — consistently leaving situations whenever panic arrives is accommodation. It must be balanced with gradual re-entry into those situations when the person is ready. [104]

    Driving panic: If panic begins while the person is driving, calmly suggest pulling over — "There's a spot right there, want to pull over for a second?" rather than any suggestion that something is wrong. If you are in the passenger seat, model calm breathing visibly. Do not reach for the steering wheel unless there is actual danger — this dramatically increases the sense of loss of control. Once pulled over, the same five-step protocol applies: presence, one sentence, modeled breath, regulate yourself, minimal post-processing.

    Nocturnal panic (waking from sleep in panic): Being woken from sleep into a panic attack is particularly disorienting because the person wakes into a state of alarm with no clear context. A calm, low voice saying their name, a hand on their arm, and one clear sentence ("You're okay, you're home, I'm here") is the priority. Turn on a warm, dim light — not a bright overhead light which will fully activate the visual cortex and make orientation harder. Keep conversation minimal until full waking orientation is achieved. This typically takes 30-60 seconds. After orientation, the standard five-step protocol continues. [101]

    15
    Communication
    What to Say — Communication Scripts for Every Situation
    ▾

    Most wingmen find the communication dimension the hardest. The three-pillar framework gives you clear things to do — food changes, movement invitations, environment adjustments. What to say is more nuanced, because what sounds supportive and what is actually supportive in panic disorder often diverge sharply. This section provides specific language for specific situations. [14]

    The Core Communication Principles

    • Acknowledge without amplifying: Validate the experience without feeding the panic loop. "I can see that's uncomfortable" works. "I know, it's terrifying, it must be awful" amplifies. [105]
    • Normalize without dismissing: "Panic attacks happen, this is the panic disorder doing its thing" normalizes. "You have nothing to worry about" dismisses. The difference is between acknowledging the physiological reality and minimizing the experience.
    • Invite without pressuring: "I'm going for a walk — feel like coming?" is an invitation. "You really should get some exercise, it would help" is pressure. The person's nervous system needs choice and autonomy (the experience of having real choice and control) — pressure activates the exact threat system you're trying to calm. [17]
    • Reduce reassurance-seeking cycles: When the person asks "Am I going to be okay?" — the reassurance-avoiding response is: "You've felt this way before and it's always passed. What would help right now?" This redirects toward coping rather than providing a temporary answer that will need to be asked again minutes later. [14]
    ✓ What to Say — Effective Communication
    • "I'm right here." [during panic]
    • "This will pass." [during panic — once, not repeated]
    • "I'm going for a walk — want to come?"
    • "I made [food] — want some?"
    • "No pressure either way."
    • "What would feel good right now?"
    • "That sounds really hard."
    • "You've gotten through this before."
    • "I'm proud of you for going." [after a difficult outing]
    • "Take your time."
    ✗ What Not to Say — Responses That Make Things Worse
    • "Calm down." [impossible instruction, adds shame]
    • "There's nothing to worry about." [dismisses experience]
    • "You're being irrational." [accurate but unhelpful]
    • "Everyone gets anxious." [minimizes severity]
    • "Just push through it." [ignores physiological reality]
    • "You need to try harder." [blame, shame, worsens outcomes]
    • "Not this again." [expressed criticism, documented predictor of worse outcomes]
    • "What's the worst that can happen?" [cognitive argument during panic]
    • "I don't know how much more of this I can take." [shifts burden to person]
    • Repeated "Are you okay? Are you sure?" [reassurance-seeking amplifier]

    Specific Situation Scripts

    Scripts for Situations That Wingmen Frequently Find Difficult

    When the person declines a social event or activity:
    Instead of: "You really should come. You can't keep avoiding everything."
    Try: "Okay, no worries. I'll fill you in when I get back." Then go. Living your normal life while leaving the door fully open — without guilt, without pressure, without disappointment — is both healthy for you and actually more therapeutic for them than guilt-based accommodation.

    When asked for the fifth time "Will I be okay?":
    Instead of: "Yes, of course, you'll be fine."
    Try: "I think you will be. And you've gotten through this before — what helped last time?" This acknowledges the question without giving the reassurance that maintains the cycle, and redirects toward self-efficacy (the person's own evidence base for handling it).

    When the person says "I can't do this anymore":
    Instead of: "Yes you can!" (dismissive) or extensive distress-sharing (amplifying).
    Try: "I hear you. This is really hard." Pause. "What would make right now a little more okay?" Focus on the immediate, manageable, present moment rather than the global statement about the future. The "can't do this anymore" is a moment's overwhelm, not an accurate prediction — respond to the emotion, not the literal statement.

    When a panic attack happens in front of family or guests:
    Stay near the person. Say quietly to anyone watching: "Give us a few minutes" — a single phrase that removes the audience. Reduce the stimulation field. Never explain the person's condition to others in that moment — this can feel humiliating and adds social shame to physiological panic. Explanations, if wanted, can happen later and should be driven by the person's own preferences about privacy.

    When asking about starting therapy:
    Instead of: "You really need to see someone."
    Try: "I read something about a therapist approach that's specifically designed for panic. Would you be open to hearing about it?" Frame it as information you found, not a verdict about what they need. Keep the framing gentle and the offer not repeated at that moment — planting the idea without pressure is more likely to result in eventual agreement than insisting.

    16
    Mistakes
    The 10 Most Common Wingman Mistakes — Why Instinct Is Wrong and What to Do Instead
    ▾

    The research on family accommodation and expressed emotion has mapped the specific patterns that make panic disorder worse when families are involved. Every one of these ten mistakes is driven by completely understandable human impulses — love, protectiveness, frustration, or simple exhaustion. And every one of them makes the disorder harder to recover from. [30]

    1

    Mistake: Endless reassurance

    What it looks like: Answering "Am I going to be okay?" the same way every time with "Yes, you're fine." Providing extensive reassurance about physical symptoms during panic. Checking in repeatedly after a panic attack to make sure the person is alright.

    Why it makes things worse: Each reassurance temporarily reduces anxiety — which reinforces the asking behavior and delays the person from learning they can tolerate uncertainty themselves. Reassurance-seeking is one of the core maintenance behaviors of anxiety disorders. Every reassurance strengthens the next reassurance-seeking request. [14]

    What to do instead: "You've felt this way before and it's always passed. What would help right now?" Redirect to coping, not reassurance. Reduce reassurance frequency gradually — don't stop cold turkey, as that itself can increase distress.

    2

    Mistake: Modifying family life around avoidance

    What it looks like: Choosing restaurants, vacation destinations, social events, and family activities based on what the person with panic disorder will be comfortable with, rather than what the family actually wants.

    Why it makes things worse: Every accommodation confirms that the avoided situation is genuinely dangerous. The avoidance is rewarded with comfort, reinforcing it. The person's world shrinks — not just their tolerance for anxiety, but their actual life territory. [30]

    What to do instead: Maintain your family's normal life, including activities the person may decline. Always invite — never exclude, never assume. When they decline, accept it warmly and without pressure. When they go — celebrate (quietly, appropriately — not making it a big deal, which adds performance pressure). Their declining is avoidance. Your going anyway, warmly, maintains the option of them going next time.

    3

    Mistake: Taking over tasks the person has stopped doing

    What it looks like: Driving everywhere because the person has stopped driving. Making all phone calls. Handling all social situations. Managing all errands that involve leaving the house.

    Why it makes things worse: Every task the wingman takes over confirms to the person that they genuinely cannot do it — and reduces their competence and self-efficacy in that area. The world of things the wingman does and the person doesn't do expands steadily. [31]

    What to do instead: Offer to do tasks together, then gradually step back. "I'll drive this time — want to try driving on the way back?" Reduce your support in the direction of the person doing more, not less. This must be done gradually and with warmth, not abruptly.

    4

    Mistake: Expressed criticism or hostility

    What it looks like: Sighing heavily when plans are canceled due to panic. Saying "Not this again." Making comments about how anxiety is affecting the family. Expressing frustration or resentment directly to the person about their disorder.

    Why it makes things worse: Expressed criticism is one of the strongest predictors of worse mental health outcomes in the research literature — stronger than many clinical variables. The person with panic disorder's nervous system is already hypervigilant to threats; criticism and hostility from a primary attachment figure (a person the brain considers critical to safety and survival) is a profound stressor that directly worsens anxiety. [29]

    What to do instead: Process frustration with a therapist, a trusted friend, or in an Al-Anon-style support setting — not with the person with panic disorder. Your feelings are real and valid. They do not belong in that particular relationship in that particular way.

    5

    Mistake: Making the disorder the center of every conversation

    What it looks like: Asking daily "How's your anxiety today?" Regularly checking in on panic attack frequency. Discussing treatment options and strategies frequently. Making every social interaction partly about the person's mental health.

    Why it makes things worse: Constant focus on the disorder increases the person's self-identification with it — making panic disorder more central to their identity and harder to separate themselves from. It also increases hypervigilance to interoceptive cues (constant body scanning for symptoms). [101]

    What to do instead: Have regular conversations about everything else in both of your lives. The disorder is part of the picture — not the picture. Let the person bring it up when they want to discuss it. Check in periodically but not constantly.

    6

    Mistake: Emotional overinvolvement

    What it looks like: Being visibly distressed by the person's distress — crying during their panic attacks, expressing how much you worry about them, making their wellbeing the primary driver of your emotional state.

    Why it makes things worse: Emotional overinvolvement is the third component of expressed emotion (alongside criticism and hostility) and is equally predictive of worse outcomes. When the wingman is visibly more upset than the person themselves during a panic attack, the person now has both their own panic and the wingman's distress to manage simultaneously — and may learn to suppress or hide panic symptoms to protect the wingman from distress. [29]

    What to do instead: Develop and maintain your own emotional regulation practice. Therapy for yourself is not optional if you are heavily emotionally entangled with the person's disorder. Your job is to be a steady, warm presence — not to feel the disorder alongside them.

    7

    Mistake: Ignoring your own wellbeing

    What it looks like: Canceling your own social plans to be available. Giving up activities you love because they seem incompatible with the person's needs. Sleeping badly from worrying. Neglecting your own medical and mental health needs.

    Why it makes things worse: A depleted, resentful, exhausted wingman cannot maintain the calm, consistent presence that the co-regulation research shows is most helpful. Burnout produces exactly the inconsistency, frustration, and emotional reactivity that damages outcomes. [4]

    What to do instead: Protect your own wellbeing as non-negotiable infrastructure for effective support. Your social life, exercise, sleep, and mental health treatment are not indulgences — they are the fuel source for everything else you are trying to do.

    8

    Mistake: Trying to reason someone out of panic

    What it looks like: During a panic attack, explaining why the situation is safe. Pointing out that the physical sensations can't hurt them. Arguing with the catastrophic thinking cognitively while the panic is happening.

    Why it makes things worse: Rational thinking requires the prefrontal cortex (the brain's reasoning center). During a panic attack, the amygdala (threat alarm) has flooded the system with stress hormones that functionally impair the prefrontal cortex. Reasoning with someone during panic is like trying to have a nuanced philosophical discussion with someone who is sprinting from a bear — the cognitive architecture needed isn't available. [106]

    What to do instead: Work bottom-up through the body. Breath, presence, and environment change the physiology. Physiology is what drove the panic, and physiology (not reasoning) is what will end it.

    9

    Mistake: Treating every difficult emotion as a panic emergency

    What it looks like: Running through the panic protocol every time the person seems anxious, sad, frustrated, or worried — treating all emotional distress as a potential panic attack that requires management.

    Why it makes things worse: This reinforces the idea that any negative emotion is dangerous and requires external management — the exact opposite of the self-efficacy (belief in one's own ability to handle challenges) building that recovery requires. [107]

    What to do instead: Distinguish between distress (uncomfortable, manageable, part of being human) and panic (the specific physiological emergency response). Let ordinary distress be ordinary. Reserve active support for genuine panic. Allow the person to experience and manage non-panic negative emotions independently.

    10

    Mistake: All-or-nothing thinking about recovery

    What it looks like: Measuring success only by whether panic attacks stopped completely. Treating any regression as failure. Getting discouraged and abandoning the framework after a hard week.

    Why it makes things worse: Recovery from panic disorder is not linear. Good weeks and bad weeks exist throughout the recovery trajectory. All-or-nothing evaluation produces the kind of expressed frustration and criticism — when expectations aren't met — that the research consistently shows worsens outcomes. [108]

    What to do instead: Measure direction over time, not daily performance. The question is not "did we have a panic-free week?" — it is "are there more good days now than six months ago? Is the person doing more, avoiding less, requiring less accommodation?" The trajectory matters. Not the daily score.
